topics.title: Sieve Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL408 - GEOTECHNICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Sieve Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Standard Definition**
> **Sieve Analysis** is a fundamental mechanical grain-size distribution test used in geotechnical engineering to determine the proportions of various particle sizes present in a soil sample. The test is performed by passing a known dry mass of soil through a stack of progressively finer sieves arranged in descending order of aperture size, and then measuring the mass retained on each sieve. The results are expressed as the percentage of the total mass passing (finer than) or retained (coarser than) each sieve size, and are graphically represented as a **semi-logarithmic grain-size distribution curve**.

The test is governed by **IS 2720 (Part 4) – 1985 (Reaffirmed 2020): "Methods of Test for Soils – Part 4 Grain Size Analysis"** and is the primary classification procedure for **coarse-grained soils** (gravel and sand) where particles are larger than **75 µm (0.075 mm)**. Particles finer than 75 µm (silts and clays) are analysed using the **sedimentation (hydrometer) method** (IS 2720 Part 4 – Sedimentation Principle).

> [!NOTE] **Key Terminology — Remember These For Exams**
> - **Particle Size (D)**: The smallest sieve aperture through which a particle can pass. Usually expressed in **mm** or **µm**.
> - **Sieve Aperture**: The clear opening between the wires of the sieve mesh. Standard sieves follow a defined geometric progression.
> - **Mass Retained ($M_r$)**: The weight of soil that stays on top of a given sieve after shaking.
> - **Cumulative Mass Retained ($\Sigma M_r$)**: Sum of masses retained on a given sieve and all coarser sieves above it.
> - **Percentage Finer ($N$)**: The percentage of soil passing through a given sieve (plotted on the y-axis of the grain-size curve).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Kitchen Strainer

Imagine you are making pasta in your kitchen. You pour the entire packet (a mixture of long spaghetti, small macaroni, and powdered dust) into a **colander** (a large strainer with big holes). The long pieces stay in the colander, the smaller macaroni falls through, and the fine powder passes all the way through into the sink. By repeating this process with progressively smaller strainers stacked one above the other, you can **separate and quantify** each size group.

Sieve analysis works on exactly this principle — a stack of sieves with progressively smaller openings acts like a series of strainers. The soil is poured onto the **top (largest) sieve**, the entire stack is mechanically shaken on a **sieve shaker**, and each sieve "catches" only the particles too big to pass through its mesh. By weighing what is caught at each level, the engineer can mathematically reconstruct the full **size distribution** of the soil.

> [!TIP] **Why Bother Doing This?**
> Grain-size distribution is the single most important property that controls:
> - **Soil classification** (whether a soil is well-graded, poorly graded, gap-graded, uniform)
> - **Permeability** (coarse soils drain faster than fine soils)
> - **Shear strength and stability** of foundations
> - **Filter design** in earth dams and retaining structures
> - **Frost susceptibility** and **liquefaction potential** assessment

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics Behind Sieving

When a stack of sieves is placed on a mechanical sieve shaker, the apparatus imparts a **complex three-dimensional motion** — vertical jolting combined with horizontal rotation. This motion has two key effects on the soil sample sitting on the top sieve:

1. **Coning and Stratification**: The vibration causes the soil mass to spread out and form a cone. Larger, heavier particles migrate to the **bottom of the layer** (where the mesh is), and finer particles ride to the top.
2. **Particle-Mesh Interception**: A particle will pass through a square mesh only when its **smallest cross-sectional dimension** is smaller than the aperture opening. For elongated or flaky particles (like mica), the particle may pass through sideways even if its longest dimension is larger than the aperture.

The shaking duration is standardized at a minimum of **10 minutes** (per IS 2720 Part 4) to ensure near-complete separation. Over-shaking is generally not a problem, but **under-shaking** leads to inaccurate results because fines remain trapped above coarser sieves.

## 2.2 The Step-by-Step Procedure Logic

The procedure is broken down into a precise sequence of operations. Each step has a clear engineering purpose:

- **Step 1 — Sample Preparation**: The soil is oven-dried at **105 °C to 110 °C** for **16 to 24 hours** to drive off all moisture. Moisture causes fine particles to stick to coarse ones (apparent cohesion), preventing proper separation.
- **Step 2 — Weighing the Total Sample**: The dried sample is accurately weighed to the nearest **0.1 g** (let this mass be $M$).
- **Step 3 — Stack Assembly**: Sieves are stacked in **descending order of aperture** — the largest opening on top, the smallest (typically **75 µm** or No. 200) at the bottom, with a **receiver pan (catch pan)** below that.
- **Step 4 — Sieve Shaking**: The stack is mounted on the sieve shaker and operated for **10 to 15 minutes**.
- **Step 5 — Weighing the Retained Mass**: Each sieve (including the pan) is weighed individually. The mass retained on any sieve is:

$$
M_r = (\text{Mass of sieve} + \text{soil retained}) - (\text{Mass of empty sieve})
$$

- **Step 6 — Loss Check**: The sum of all retained masses plus the pan mass is compared with the original total mass $M$. The loss **must not exceed 1 %** of the original mass per IS 2720. Excessive loss indicates material flew out during shaking or adhered to sieve walls.
- **Step 7 — Computation & Plotting**: The cumulative percentage retained and the percentage finer are calculated for each sieve and plotted on a **semi-log graph** (sieve size on log-x axis, percentage finer on arithmetic y-axis).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **The following table is a MUST-MEMORIZE cheat sheet for KTU exams.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|----------|---------|----------------|------|
| 1 | Mass Retained on sieve $i$ | $M_{r,i} = M_{sieve+soil,i} - M_{empty,i}$ | Mass difference method | **g** |
| 2 | Percentage Retained on sieve $i$ | $\% R_i = \dfrac{M_{r,i}}{M} \times 100$ | $M$ = total dry mass | **%** |
| 3 | Cumulative Percentage Retained | $\Sigma \% R_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} \% R_j$ | Running sum down the stack | **%** |
| 4 | Percentage Finer (Passing) | $N_i = 100 - \Sigma \% R_i$ | Plot this on y-axis | **%** |
| 5 | Coefficient of Uniformity | $C_u = \dfrac{D_{60}}{D_{10}}$ | $D_{60}$ = size at 60 % finer | dimensionless |
| 6 | Coefficient of Curvature | $C_c = \dfrac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \times D_{60}}$ | $D_{30}$ = size at 30 % finer | dimensionless |
| 7 | Fineness Modulus (FM) | $FM = \dfrac{\sum \Sigma \% R_i \text{ (from 150 µm to 4.75 mm)}}{100}$ | Sum of cumulative % retained on standard sieves | dimensionless |

> [!TIP] **Classification Cut-Offs (IS Standard Sieve Sizes)**
> - **Gravel (G)**: Coarser than **4.75 mm** (retained on 4.75 mm / No. 4 sieve)
> - **Sand (S)**: Between **4.75 mm and 75 µm** (No. 4 to No. 200)
> - **Silt + Clay (M + C)**: Finer than **75 µm** (passing No. 200)
> - **Coarse Sand**: 4.75 mm – 2.00 mm
> - **Medium Sand**: 2.00 mm – 425 µm
> - **Fine Sand**: 425 µm – 75 µm

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sieve analysis is the **first test** performed on any soil sample arriving at a geotechnical lab. Its applications are vast:

- **Highway and Railway Subgrade Design**: Pavement design requires knowing the percentage of fines because they control swelling and frost heave behaviour.
- **Earthen Dam Construction**: The filter zones around the impervious core must satisfy **Terzaghi's filter criteria**:
$$
\dfrac{D_{15,\text{filter}}}{D_{85,\text{base}}} \le 4 \quad \text{to} \quad 5
$$
$$
\dfrac{D_{15,\text{filter}}}{D_{15,\text{base}}} \ge 4 \quad \text{to} \quad 5
$$
  These ratios are extracted directly from grain-size curves.
- **Concrete Aggregate Grading**: Coarse and fine aggregates used in concrete must lie within specified grading envelopes (IS 383), and sieve analysis is the verification tool.
- **Filter Media Design for Water Treatment Plants**: Rapid sand filters use sand with $D_{10}$ between **0.45 mm and 0.7 mm** — directly read off the grain-size curve.
- **Soil Identification & Classification** (ISCS / USCS): A soil's group symbol (SW, SP, SM, SC, GW, GP, GM, GC) is decided by combining its grain-size data and Atterberg limits.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Percentage Finer Equation

Let $M$ be the **total dry mass** of the soil sample in grams. Let $M_{r,1}, M_{r,2}, \ldots, M_{r,n}$ be the masses retained on sieves numbered from **top to bottom** (i.e., sieve 1 is the coarsest with the largest aperture, sieve $n$ is the finest with the smallest aperture, and an additional "sieve $n+1$" is the receiver pan).

**Step 1 — Percentage retained on a single sieve $i$:**

By definition, the percentage of the original sample that is retained on sieve $i$ is the mass retained divided by the total mass, expressed as a percentage:

$$
\% R_i = \dfrac{M_{r,i}}{M} \times 100
$$

This is the **fraction of the whole** that did not pass through sieve $i$.

**Step 2 — Cumulative percentage retained up to sieve $i$:**

To find out how much of the total sample is **coarser than** sieve $i$, we add up the percentages retained on sieve $i$ itself and on every sieve above it (which is coarser):

$$
\Sigma \% R_i = \% R_1 + \% R_2 + \ldots + \% R_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} \% R_j
$$

The "cumulative" qualifier indicates that the sum keeps increasing as we move down the sieve stack.

**Step 3 — Percentage finer than sieve $i$:**

By the **law of conservation of mass**, every grain of soil in the original sample must either be retained on some sieve (or the pan) or be coarser than the top sieve. There is no other option. Therefore:

$$
\Sigma \% R_i + \% \text{Finer}_i = 100\%
$$

Rearranging gives the master equation for the **percentage finer (passing)** value plotted on the y-axis of the grain-size curve:

$$
N_i = \% \text{Finer}_i = 100 - \Sigma \% R_i = 100 - \sum_{j=1}^{i} \% R_j
$$

This value is the **ordinate** used in plotting the grain-size distribution curve.

**Step 4 — Derivation of $D_{10}, D_{60}, D_{30}$:**

Once the grain-size curve is plotted with sieve size $D$ on the log-x-axis and $N$ (% finer) on the arithmetic y-axis, we read off the abscissa corresponding to specific ordinates:

- $D_{10}$ = sieve size (in mm) corresponding to $N = 10\,\%$ finer
- $D_{30}$ = sieve size (in mm) corresponding to $N = 30\,\%$ finer
- $D_{60}$ = sieve size (in mm) corresponding to $N = 60\,\%$ finer

These characteristic sizes feed directly into the **coefficient of uniformity** and **coefficient of curvature**:

$$
C_u = \dfrac{D_{60}}{D_{10}}
$$

$$
C_c = \dfrac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}}
$$

A **well-graded** sand (SW) requires $C_u \ge 6$ and $1 \le C_c \le 3$. A **well-graded** gravel (GW) requires $C_u \ge 4$ and $1 \le C_c \le 3$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE] **Sample Problem — Refer IS 2720 Part 4 Format**
> A dry soil sample weighing **1000 g** is subjected to sieve analysis using a stack of standard sieves. The mass retained on each sieve is given below. Compute (i) the percentage retained, (ii) the cumulative percentage retained, (iii) the percentage finer for each sieve, and (iv) the coefficient of uniformity and coefficient of curvature. Classify the soil.

| Sieve No. | Aperture (mm) | Mass Retained (g) |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| 4 | 4.75 | 50 |
| 10 | 2.00 | 100 |
| 20 | 0.85 | 200 |
| 40 | 0.425 | 250 |
| 60 | 0.250 | 175 |
| 140 | 0.106 | 120 |
| 200 | 0.075 | 55 |
| Pan | — | 50 |

**Step 1 — Total Mass Check:**
The sum of all retained masses plus pan content = $50 + 100 + 200 + 250 + 175 + 120 + 55 + 50 = 1000$ g, which exactly matches the total sample. So the loss is **0 %** (well within the 1 % limit).

**Step 2 — Compute Percentage Retained for each sieve:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% R_4 &= \dfrac{50}{1000} \times 100 = 5.0\,\% \\
\% R_{10} &= \dfrac{100}{1000} \times 100 = 10.0\,\% \\
\% R_{20} &= \dfrac{200}{1000} \times 100 = 20.0\,\% \\
\% R_{40} &= \dfrac{250}{1000} \times 100 = 25.0\,\% \\
\% R_{60} &= \dfrac{175}{1000} \times 100 = 17.5\,\% \\
\% R_{140} &= \dfrac{120}{1000} \times 100 = 12.0\,\% \\
\% R_{200} &= \dfrac{55}{1000} \times 100 = 5.5\,\% \\
\% R_{\text{pan}} &= \dfrac{50}{1000} \times 100 = 5.0\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Verification: $5.0 + 10.0 + 20.0 + 25.0 + 17.5 + 12.0 + 5.5 + 5.0 = 100.0$ %.

**Step 3 — Cumulative Percentage Retained and Percentage Finer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma \% R_4 &= 5.0 \quad \rightarrow \quad N_4 = 100 - 5.0 = 95.0\,\% \\
\Sigma \% R_{10} &= 5.0 + 10.0 = 15.0 \quad \rightarrow \quad N_{10} = 100 - 15.0 = 85.0\,\% \\
\Sigma \% R_{20} &= 15.0 + 20.0 = 35.0 \quad \rightarrow \quad N_{20} = 100 - 35.0 = 65.0\,\% \\
\Sigma \% R_{40} &= 35.0 + 25.0 = 60.0 \quad \rightarrow \quad N_{40} = 100 - 60.0 = 40.0\,\% \\
\Sigma \% R_{60} &= 60.0 + 17.5 = 77.5 \quad \rightarrow \quad N_{60} = 100 - 77.5 = 22.5\,\% \\
\Sigma \% R_{140} &= 77.5 + 12.0 = 89.5 \quad \rightarrow \quad N_{140} = 100 - 89.5 = 10.5\,\% \\
\Sigma \% R_{200} &= 89.5 + 5.5 = 95.0 \quad \rightarrow \quad N_{200} = 100 - 95.0 = 5.0\,\% \\
\Sigma \% R_{\text{pan}} &= 95.0 + 5.0 = 100.0 \quad \rightarrow \quad N_{\text{pan}} = 0.0\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Read $D_{10}, D_{30}, D_{60}$ from the curve (interpolated values):**

From the grain-size distribution curve (semi-log plot):

- $D_{60} \approx 0.55$ mm (from $N = 60\,\%$)
- $D_{30} \approx 0.32$ mm (from $N = 30\,\%$)
- $D_{10} \approx 0.085$ mm (from $N = 10\,\%$)

**Step 5 — Compute $C_u$ and $C_c$:**

$$
C_u = \dfrac{D_{60}}{D_{10}} = \dfrac{0.55}{0.085} \approx 6.47
$$

$$
C_c = \dfrac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}} = \dfrac{(0.32)^2}{0.085 \times 0.55} = \dfrac{0.1024}{0.04675} \approx 2.19
$$

**Step 6 — Classification:**

Since the soil contains **5 %** fines (passing 75 µm), which is **less than 5 %**? No, 5 % is on the boundary. With 5 % fines, the soil classifies as **Sand (S)** under ISCS. The $C_u = 6.47 \ge 6$ and $1 \le C_c = 2.19 \le 3$, so the soil is **Well-Graded Sand (SW)** under ISCS/USCS. However, since fines = 5 % (boundary), it may be **SW-SM** (well-graded sand with silt) if fines show non-plastic behaviour. Final symbol: **SW**.

## 3.3 Loss of Material — Bound Check

Per IS 2720 Part 4, the permissible loss of material is given by:

$$
\% \text{Loss} = \dfrac{M - \sum_{i=1}^{n+1} M_{r,i}}{M} \times 100 \le 1\,\%
$$

If the loss exceeds 1 %, the test is to be **repeated**. This is a strict KTU exam point — students often forget to apply this check.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Sieve Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Receive Soil Sample] --> B[Step 2: Oven Dry at 105 to 110 deg C for 16 to 24 hours]
    B --> C[Step 3: Weigh Total Dry Mass M grams]
    C --> D[Step 4: Assemble Sieve Stack Coarse on Top Fine at Bottom plus Pan]
    D --> E[Step 5: Place Stack on Sieve Shaker]
    E --> F[Step 6: Shake for 10 to 15 minutes]
    F --> G[Step 7: Weigh Each Sieve plus Retained Soil]
    G --> H[Step 8: Compute Mass Retained Mr per Sieve]
    H --> I[Step 9: Compute Percent Retained and Cumulative Percent Retained]
    I --> J[Step 10: Compute Percent Finer N equals 100 minus Cumulative]
    J --> K[Step 11: Check Material Loss less than 1 percent]
    K --> L{Loss within Limit?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Step 12: Plot Semi Log Grain Size Distribution Curve]
    L -->|No| N[Repeat Test with Fresh Sample]
    M --> O[Step 13: Read D10 D30 D60 from Curve]
    O --> P[Step 14: Compute Cu and Cc]
    P --> Q[Step 15: Classify Soil per ISCS or USCS]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Physical Apparatus Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TOP[Top of Sieve Stack]
        S1[Sieve No 4 Aperture 4.75 mm]
    end
    subgraph MID1[Middle Layer Coarse Sand]
        S2[Sieve No 10 Aperture 2.00 mm]
        S3[Sieve No 20 Aperture 0.85 mm]
        S4[Sieve No 40 Aperture 0.425 mm]
    end
    subgraph MID2[Middle Layer Fine Sand]
        S5[Sieve No 60 Aperture 0.250 mm]
        S6[Sieve No 100 Aperture 0.150 mm]
        S7[Sieve No 140 Aperture 0.106 mm]
        S8[Sieve No 200 Aperture 0.075 mm]
    end
    subgraph BOT[Bottom of Stack]
        SP[Receiver Pan Catches Silt and Clay]
    end
    SHAKER[Mechanical Sieve Shaker Motor Driven Vibratory Unit]:::machine
    TOP --> MID1 --> MID2 --> BOT
    S1 -. Mounted on .-> SHAKER
    S2 -. Mounted on .-> SHAKER
    SHAKER --> RESULT[Soil Separated by Size]
    RESULT --> MASS[Weigh Each Component]
    MASS --> PLOT[Grain Size Distribution Curve]

    classDef machine fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Layer Diagram of Particle Stratification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Largest Particles Gravel size greater than 4.75 mm] --> B[Catch on Top Sieve No 4]
    C[Coarse Sand 2.00 to 4.75 mm] --> D[Catch on Sieve No 10]
    E[Medium Sand 0.425 to 2.00 mm] --> F[Catch on Sieves No 20 and No 40]
    G[Fine Sand 0.075 to 0.425 mm] --> H[Catch on Sieves No 60 to No 200]
    I[Silt and Clay less than 0.075 mm] --> J[Collect in Receiver Pan]

    B --> K[Weigh and Record Mr1]
    D --> L[Weigh and Record Mr2]
    F --> M[Weigh and Record Mr3 Mr4]
    H --> N[Weigh and Record Mr5 to Mr8]
    J --> O[Weigh and Record Pan Mass]

    K --> P[Compute Percent Finer N for Each Sieve]
    L --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Plot Semi Log Curve and Classify Soil]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] 
**Define Sieve Analysis. State the Indian Standard code used for this test and the minimum shaking duration recommended.**

> **Model Answer:**
> Sieve Analysis is a mechanical grain-size distribution test in which a known dry mass of soil is passed through a stack of standard sieves arranged in descending order of aperture size, and the mass retained on each sieve is used to determine the percentage of soil particles of various sizes.
> - **Indian Standard Code:** IS 2720 (Part 4) — 1985 (Reaffirmed 2020)
> - **Minimum Shaking Duration:** 10 to 15 minutes on a mechanical sieve shaker
> - **Suitable for:** Soils coarser than 75 µm (gravels and sands)
> **[3 Marks]**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**What is the Coefficient of Uniformity ($C_u$) and Coefficient of Curvature ($C_c$)? State the conditions for a soil to be classified as well-graded.**

> **Model Answer:**
> $C_u = \dfrac{D_{60}}{D_{10}}$ and $C_c = \dfrac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}}$.
>
> For a soil to be **well-graded**:
> - **Gravel (GW):** $C_u \ge 4$ and $1 \le C_c \le 3$
> - **Sand (SW):** $C_u \ge 6$ and $1 \le C_c \le 3$
>
> If $C_u$ is less than the specified limit, the soil is **poorly graded (uniform)**. If $C_c$ lies outside the range 1 to 3, the soil is **gap-graded**.
> **[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model Paper]

**(a)** A dry soil sample of mass **500 g** was subjected to sieve analysis. The data is tabulated below. Plot the grain-size distribution curve and determine the percentages of gravel, sand, and fines in the soil. **[7 Marks]**

| Sieve Size (mm) | Mass Retained (g) |
|-----------------|-------------------|
| 4.75 | 25 |
| 2.00 | 50 |
| 0.425 | 100 |
| 0.150 | 175 |
| 0.075 | 75 |
| Pan | 75 |

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify Total Mass Recovery:**
$25 + 50 + 100 + 175 + 75 + 75 = 500$ g = Original mass. Loss = 0 %. Valid test.

**Step 2 — Compute Percentage Retained:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%R_{4.75} &= \tfrac{25}{500}\times 100 = 5.0\% \\
\%R_{2.00} &= \tfrac{50}{500}\times 100 = 10.0\% \\
\%R_{0.425} &= \tfrac{100}{500}\times 100 = 20.0\% \\
\%R_{0.150} &= \tfrac{175}{500}\times 100 = 35.0\% \\
\%R_{0.075} &= \tfrac{75}{500}\times 100 = 15.0\% \\
\%R_{\text{pan}} &= \tfrac{75}{500}\times 100 = 15.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Cumulative % Retained and % Finer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma \%R_{4.75} &= 5.0 \rightarrow N = 95.0\% \\
\Sigma \%R_{2.00} &= 15.0 \rightarrow N = 85.0\% \\
\Sigma \%R_{0.425} &= 35.0 \rightarrow N = 65.0\% \\
\Sigma \%R_{0.150} &= 70.0 \rightarrow N = 30.0\% \\
\Sigma \%R_{0.075} &= 85.0 \rightarrow N = 15.0\% \\
\Sigma \%R_{\text{pan}} &= 100.0 \rightarrow N = 0.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Plot the Curve:**
A semi-log plot is drawn with sieve size (mm) on log-x axis and % finer on arithmetic y-axis (0 to 100). The points are connected by a smooth curve.

**Step 5 — Composition of Soil:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{Gravel} &= 100 - N_{4.75} = 100 - 95 = 5.0\% \\
\% \text{Sand} &= N_{4.75} - N_{0.075} = 95.0 - 15.0 = 80.0\% \\
\% \text{Fines (Silt + Clay)} &= N_{0.075} = 15.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The soil contains **5 % gravel, 80 % sand, and 15 % fines**. Since fines > 12 %, plasticity tests are required before final classification, but the soil is predominantly a **silty sand (SM)**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the procedure for conducting a sieve analysis in the laboratory. List the apparatus required and the precautions to be observed. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution Outline:**

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Set of standard IS sieves (4.75 mm, 2.00 mm, 425 µm, 150 µm, 75 µm) + receiver pan and lid
2. Mechanical sieve shaker (rotary + vibratory type)
3. Sensitive electronic balance (accuracy ± 0.1 g)
4. Drying oven (105 °C – 110 °C)
5. Wire brush and brush for cleaning sieves
6. Sample dividers (riffle box) and trays

**Procedure (in steps):**
1. Take a representative oven-dried soil sample weighing about **500 g** for sandy soils, **2000 g** for gravelly soils.
2. Weigh the sample accurately to **0.1 g** — record as $M$.
3. Assemble the sieves in **descending order of aperture** — largest at top, smallest at bottom, with receiver pan at the very bottom.
4. Pour the soil onto the top sieve, fit the lid, and place the stack on the sieve shaker.
5. Run the shaker for **10 to 15 minutes**.
6. Remove the stack and weigh each sieve **individually** with its retained soil.
7. Compute mass retained, % retained, cumulative % retained, and % finer for each sieve.
8. Check material loss — must be **< 1 %** of original mass.
9. Plot the semi-log grain-size distribution curve.

**Precautions:**
- Sieves must be **clean and dry** before use; check for **damaged mesh**.
- Sample must be **completely oven-dried** before sieving.
- Do not overload sieves (max 50–100 g per sieve).
- **Weighing accuracy** of 0.1 g is mandatory.
- Handle sieves gently to **avoid spillage**.
- Material loss must not exceed 1 %; if it does, **repeat the test**. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**(a)** The grain-size analysis of a soil gave the following data. Compute $C_u$ and $C_c$ and classify the soil as well-graded, poorly graded, or gap-graded. **[7 Marks]**

| Sieve Size (mm) | Percent Finer (%) |
|-----------------|-------------------|
| 4.75 | 100 |
| 2.00 | 95 |
| 0.425 | 70 |
| 0.150 | 28 |
| 0.075 | 8 |

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Read $D_{10}, D_{30}, D_{60}$ from the curve:**

The given table provides the y-coordinates (% finer) directly. Using semi-log interpolation (or reading off the plotted curve):

- $D_{60}$ corresponds to $N = 60\,\%$ → between 0.425 mm (70 %) and 0.150 mm (28 %). Interpolating on the log scale:
$$
D_{60} = 0.425 \times \left(\dfrac{0.150}{0.425}\right)^{\tfrac{70 - 60}{70 - 28}} = 0.425 \times (0.3529)^{0.2381} \approx 0.30 \text{ mm}
$$

- $D_{30}$ corresponds to $N = 30\,\%$ → just below 0.150 mm (28 %): $D_{30} \approx 0.150$ mm.

- $D_{10}$ corresponds to $N = 10\,\%$ → between 0.150 mm (28 %) and 0.075 mm (8 %):
$$
D_{10} = 0.150 \times \left(\dfrac{0.075}{0.150}\right)^{\tfrac{28 - 10}{28 - 8}} = 0.150 \times (0.5)^{0.90} \approx 0.0786 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_u$:**

$$
C_u = \dfrac{D_{60}}{D_{10}} = \dfrac{0.30}{0.0786} \approx 3.82
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_c$:**

$$
C_c = \dfrac{(D_{30})^2}{D_{10} \cdot D_{60}} = \dfrac{(0.150)^2}{0.0786 \times 0.30} = \dfrac{0.0225}{0.02358} \approx 0.954
$$

**Step 4 — Classification:**

For a **well-graded sand**, we need $C_u \ge 6$ AND $1 \le C_c \le 3$.

Here, $C_u = 3.82 < 6$ and $C_c = 0.954 < 1$ — **both criteria fail**.

Since the soil is **sandy** (predominantly between 75 µm and 4.75 mm) and does not satisfy well-graded conditions, the soil is classified as **Poorly Graded Sand (SP)** under ISCS / USCS. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** What is Fineness Modulus? How is it computed from sieve analysis data? State its significance in concrete mix design. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
Fineness Modulus (FM) is an empirical factor obtained by adding the **cumulative percentages of material retained** on each of a specified series of sieves (from 150 µm No. 100 to 4.75 mm No. 4) and dividing the sum by 100.

**Computation:**

$$
FM = \dfrac{\sum \text{Cumulative \% Retained on Standard Sieves}}{100}
$$

The standard sieves used for FM calculation (per IS 383) are: 4.75 mm, 2.36 mm, 1.18 mm, 600 µm, 300 µm, and 150 µm.

**Example:** If cumulative % retained on these sieves are 2, 12, 30, 55, 78, 95, then:

$$
FM = \dfrac{2 + 12 + 30 + 55 + 78 + 95}{100} = \dfrac{272}{100} = 2.72
$$

**Significance in Concrete Mix Design:**
- FM gives a **single-number index** of the fineness of aggregate — higher FM = coarser aggregate.
- For **fine aggregate (sand)**, the recommended FM range is **2.2 to 3.2** as per IS 383.
- For **coarse aggregate**, the FM is typically between **6.0 and 8.0**.
- FM is used to **control the grading** of aggregate supplied to a batching plant — sudden change in FM signals a change in source material and possible strength/workability issues.
- It is also used in the **Abrams' mix design method** and the **ACI method** to proportion fine and coarse aggregate. **[7 Marks]**

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Forgetting the Loss Check:** Always verify that the sum of retained masses + pan mass equals the original total mass within 1 %. Examiners explicitly look for this line.
> - **Misidentifying $D_{10}, D_{30}, D_{60}$:** These are the sieve sizes at $N = 10\%$, $N = 30\%$, $N = 60\%$ respectively, NOT the percentage retained values. This is a recurring mistake.
> - **Wrong Plot Type:** The grain-size curve is a **semi-log plot** (sieve size on log-axis, % finer on arithmetic axis). A linear-linear plot will lose all marks.
> - **Sand vs. Gravel Boundary:** Students often confuse the boundary — anything **retained on 4.75 mm sieve** is gravel; anything **passing 4.75 mm but retained on 75 µm** is sand; anything **passing 75 µm** is fines.
> - **Decimal Errors in % Finer:** Each arithmetic step must be shown. Skipping the cumulative % retained column will cost you 2–3 marks.
> - **Forgetting to state the IS code:** Always cite **IS 2720 (Part 4) — 1985 (Reaffirmed 2020)** when describing the test.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- **Definition**: Sieve analysis determines the grain-size distribution of coarse-grained soils by passing a known dry mass through a stack of standard sieves. **Code: IS 2720 Part 4**.
- **Particle boundary sizes**: Gravel > 4.75 mm; Sand = 0.075 mm to 4.75 mm; Fines (silt + clay) < 0.075 mm.
- **Core formulas** (memorize verbatim):
    - $\%R_i = (M_{r,i}/M) \times 100$
    - $\Sigma\%R_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} \%R_j$
    - $N_i = 100 - \Sigma\%R_i$ ← **percentage finer (plotted on y-axis)**
- **Coefficients of grading**:
    - $C_u = D_{60}/D_{10}$
    - $C_c = (D_{30})^2/(D_{10} \cdot D_{60})$
- **Well-graded criteria**: Sand needs $C_u \ge 6$, $1 \le C_c \le 3$; Gravel needs $C_u \ge 4$, $1 \le C_c \le 3$.
- **Fineness Modulus**: Sum of cumulative % retained on standard sieves (150 µm to 4.75 mm) divided by 100. Range 2.2–3.2 for fine aggregate in concrete.
- **Plot type**: **Semi-log** — log-x (sieve size in mm), linear-y (% finer, 0 to 100).
- **Sample size**: 500 g for sandy soils, 2000 g for gravelly soils.
- **Oven drying**: 105 °C to 110 °C for 16 to 24 hours.
- **Shaking duration**: 10 to 15 minutes.
- **Loss check**: Sum of retained masses must match original mass within **1 %**.
- **Sieve stacking order**: Coarse at top, fine at bottom, pan at very bottom, lid on top.
- **Apparatus**: Sieve set, mechanical sieve shaker, electronic balance (0.1 g accuracy), oven, brush, riffle box.
- **Real-world uses**: Soil classification, pavement subgrade design, filter design in earth dams, concrete aggregate grading, water treatment filter sand selection.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Specific Gravity - Pycnometer and Specific Gravity Bottle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCVL408 - GEOTECHNICAL ENGINEERING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Determination of Specific Gravity - Pycnometer and Specific Gravity Bottle ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (As per IS 2720 – Part 3) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Specific Gravity of Soil Solids (Gs or G)** is defined as the ratio of the weight (or mass) of a given volume of soil solids to the weight (or mass) of an equal volume of distilled water at a standard reference temperature of **27 °C** (as per Indian Standard IS 2720: Part 3 – 1980, reaffirmed 2002). Mathematically, it is a dimensionless quantity: $$G_s = \frac{W_s}{V_s \cdot \gamma_w} = \frac{\text{Unit weight of soil solids}}{\text{Unit weight of water at 27 °C}}$$ where, - $W_s$ = Weight of soil solids - $V_s$ = Volume of soil solids - $\gamma_w$ = Unit weight of distilled water at 27 °C (≈ **9.81 kN/m³**, density ρw ≈ **0.997 g/cm³**) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Mug" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Imagine a sealed, oddly shaped ceramic mug filled completely with dry coffee powder.** You want to know how heavy the coffee powder itself is compared to water that would occupy the same space. You cannot just dunk the powder in water (it floats, absorbs, or leaks), and you cannot compute its volume from its irregular shape using a ruler. > > **The Pycnometer solves this beautifully:** It is essentially a precision "scientific mug" with a perfectly ground, leak-proof stopper containing a tiny capillary hole. By weighing the mug empty, then with dry powder, then with powder + water filled to the brim, we mathematically cancel out the mug's own weight and volume — leaving us with nothing but the powder's intrinsic heaviness vs. water. This is exactly what the pycnometer does for soil grains, but the **trick** is the **vacuum step** — to suck out the invisible air bubbles clinging between the tiny soil particles that would otherwise make our measurement falsely low. ### 1.3 Engineering Significance of Specific Gravity | Engineering Use | Why Gs Matters | |---|---| | **Void Ratio (e) Computation** | Used in $e = \frac{G_s \cdot \rho_w}{\rho_d} - 1$ | | **Degree of Saturation** | Core input in $S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s$ | | **Particle Density** | Indicator of mineralogy (quartz, mica, organic matter) | | **Soil Classification** | Low Gs (≈ 1.0–2.0) signals organic/peat soils | | **Compaction & Phase Relations** | Required to convert field density to dry density | > [!NOTE] > **Typical Range of Specific Gravity Values (KTU Reference):** > - **Sands:** 2.65 – 2.67 (predominantly quartz) > - **Silts:** 2.67 – 2.70 > - **Clays:** 2.70 – 2.80 > - **Organic Soils / Peat:** < 2.00 (often 1.0 – 1.6) > - **Soils with heavy minerals (iron, mica):** > 2.80 > - A default value of **Gs = 2.65** is used for coarse-grained soils when direct testing is not feasible. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Underlying Principle — A "Triple Weighing" Mass Balance The pycnometer method does **not** measure volume geometrically. It uses a **volumetric displacement** technique based on the **Archimedes principle**, but applied indirectly through mass conservation. **The Big Idea:** If we know the *mass of water that perfectly fills the empty bottle* and the *mass of water that fills the bottle when soil is already inside*, then the **difference** is exactly the mass of water displaced by the soil solids — which equals the mass of an equal volume of water. ### 2.2 Step-Wise Logical Breakdown 1. **Empty bottle weight** ($W_1$) → Captures the *tare* of the container. 2. **Bottle + dry soil** ($W_2$) → Subtract $W_1$ to get **true mass of soil solids** ($M_s = W_2 - W_1$). 3. **Bottle + soil + water (filled to the mark)** ($W_3$) → This mass contains the soil *plus only as much water as can fit around the grains*. 4. **Bottle + water only (filled to the mark)** ($W_4$) → This is the **calibration** of the bottle — tells us the *total volumetric capacity* of the bottle in terms of equivalent water mass. 5. **Mathematical cancellation** removes both the bottle's mass and its volume, isolating only the soil property. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield, Exam-Ready) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **single most important reference** for this experiment. Memorize the notations — KTU examiners use the same symbols in viva and ESE questions. | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit | |---|---|---| | $W_1$ | Mass of empty, clean, dry pycnometer | grams (g) | | $W_2$ | Mass of pycnometer + oven-dry soil | grams (g) | | $W_3$ | Mass of pycnometer + soil + distilled water (full) | grams (g) | | $W_4$ | Mass of pycnometer + distilled water only (full) | grams (g) | | $T$ | Test temperature of contents (during $W_3$, $W_4$) | °C | #### Master Equation (IS 2720 Part 3) $$\boxed{\,G_s \;=\; \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{\,(W_4 - W_1)\,-\,(W_3 - W_2)\,}\,}$$ #### Equivalent Rearranged Form (Frequently Used in KTU Solutions) $$G_s \;=\; \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{\,(W_2 - W_1)\,+\,(W_4 - W_3)\,}$$ > Both forms are **algebraically identical**. The second form is often easier to remember because it groups the soil mass and the "missing water" mass directly. #### Temperature Correction to 27 °C If the weighing is performed at any other temperature $T$ (°C), the corrected specific gravity is: $$G_{s,27} \;=\; G_{s,T} \;\times\; \frac{\rho_{w,T}}{\rho_{w,27}}$$ Common density of water values (ρw, g/cm³): | Temperature (°C) | 15 | 20 | 25 | 27 | 30 | 35 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $\rho_w$ (g/cm³) | 0.9991 | 0.9982 | 0.9970 | 0.9965 | 0.9956 | 0.9940 | #### Derived Volume Relation $$V_s = \frac{M_s}{G_s \cdot \rho_w} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad e = \frac{G_s \cdot \rho_w}{\rho_d} - 1$$ > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Specific gravity is the *keystone* in deriving all other **phase relations** in soil mechanics. Without an accurate Gs, the void ratio, degree of saturation, and relative density computations become erroneous — leading to unsafe bearing capacity or settlement predictions in foundation design. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Master Equation We begin with the **fundamental definition** of specific gravity as a ratio of masses (or weights) occupying identical volumes: $$G_s = \frac{\text{Mass of soil solids}}{\text{Mass of an equal volume of water}}$$ #### Step 1 — Express the mass of soil solids The pycnometer is weighed empty, and then with dry soil inside: $$M_s = W_2 - W_1$$ where, - $W_1$ = mass of the empty, clean, dry pycnometer - $W_2$ = mass of the pycnometer + oven-dry soil sample #### Step 2 — Express the volumetric capacity of the pycnometer When the pycnometer is filled to the calibration mark with **distilled water only**, the mass of water it contains is: $$M_{w,\text{full}} = W_4 - W_1$$ Since the density of water $\rho_w$ is known, the **total volume of the pycnometer bottle** is: $$V_{\text{bottle}} = \frac{M_{w,\text{full}}}{\rho_w} = \frac{W_4 - W_1}{\rho_w}$$ #### Step 3 — Express the mass of water that fills the *remaining* space when soil is inside When the pycnometer contains the soil *and* is then topped up with water to the same calibration mark, the mass of water now present is: $$M_{w,\text{partial}} = W_3 - W_2$$ because $W_3$ includes the bottle, the dry soil, and the added water. The **volume occupied by this water** is: $$V_{w,\text{partial}} = \frac{M_{w,\text{partial}}}{\rho_w} = \frac{W_3 - W_2}{\rho_w}$$ #### Step 4 — Compute the volume of soil solids The volume of soil solids occupies the space that water can no longer enter inside the bottle: $$V_s = V_{\text{bottle}} - V_{w,\text{partial}}$$ Substituting from Steps 2 and 3: $$V_s = \frac{W_4 - W_1}{\rho_w} - \frac{W_3 - W_2}{\rho_w}$$ $$V_s = \frac{\,(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)\,}{\rho_w}$$ #### Step 5 — Compute the mass of water equal in volume to the soil solids Using the fundamental property $\text{mass} = \text{volume} \times \text{density}$: $$M_{w,\text{equal volume}} = V_s \cdot \rho_w = (W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)$$ Notice the elegant result: **the $\rho_w$ cancels out completely** — this is why specific gravity is dimensionless. #### Step 6 — Form the final ratio By definition: $$G_s = \frac{M_s}{M_{w,\text{equal volume}}}$$ Substituting the expressions from Step 1 and Step 5: $$\boxed{\,G_s = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{\,(W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2)\,}\,}$$ **Algebraic equivalence check** — alternative form: $$ \begin{aligned} (W_4 - W_1) - (W_3 - W_2) &= W_4 - W_1 - W_3 + W_2 \\ &= (W_2 - W_1) + (W_4 - W_3) \end{aligned} $$ Therefore, the equivalent compact form is: $$G_s = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{\,(W_2 - W_1) + (W_4 - W_3)\,}$$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard) > [!NOTE] > **Problem (ESE Model):** In a pycnometer test on a sandy soil sample, the following observations were recorded at a test temperature of 27 °C: > > - Mass of empty pycnometer, $W_1$ = **480 g** > - Mass of pycnometer + dry soil, $W_2$ = **880 g** > - Mass of pycnometer + soil + water (full), $W_3$ = **1510 g** > - Mass of pycnometer + water only (full), $W_4$ = **1280 g** > > Determine the **specific gravity of the soil solids**. #### Solution — Step by Step **Step A — Compute mass of dry soil** $$M_s = W_2 - W_1 = 880 - 480 = 400 \text{ g}$$ **Step B — Compute mass of water that fills the empty pycnometer** $$M_{w,\text{full}} = W_4 - W_1 = 1280 - 480 = 800 \text{ g}$$ **Step C — Compute mass of water present with soil in the pycnometer** $$M_{w,\text{partial}} = W_3 - W_2 = 1510 - 880 = 630 \text{ g}$$ **Step D — Compute mass of water displaced by the soil solids** $$M_{w,\text{displaced}} = M_{w,\text{full}} - M_{w,\text{partial}} = 800 - 630 = 170 \text{ g}$$ **Step E — Form the specific gravity ratio** $$G_s = \frac{M_s}{M_{w,\text{displaced}}} = \frac{400}{170} = 2.353$$ **Step F — Apply temperature correction (if needed)** Since $T = 27$ °C, no correction is necessary. #### Final Result $$\boxed{\,G_s = 2.35\,}$$ **Verification using the alternative formula:** $$G_s = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{(W_2 - W_1) + (W_4 - W_3)} = \frac{400}{400 + (1280 - 1510)} = \frac{400}{400 - 230} = \frac{400}{170} = 2.353 \;\; \checkmark$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity Check:** The value 2.35 is **lower than typical** for sandy soil (expected range 2.65 – 2.67). In practice, this would suggest the presence of organic matter, an under-de-aired sample, or a calibration error. A repeat test with longer vacuum application (15–20 min) is recommended. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Apparatus Setup — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Oven-Dried Soil Sample
100 to 500 g at 105 to 110 C] --> B[Clean Dry Pycnometer
500 mL capacity] B --> C[Weighing Balance
Accuracy 0.01 g] C --> D[Record W1 and W2] D --> E[Add Distilled Water
Cover soil by 25 mm] E --> F[Apply Vacuum
10 to 20 minutes or Boil gently] F --> G[Top up with Distilled Water
to calibration mark] G --> H[Weigh and Record W3
at test temperature T] H --> I[Empty and Rinse Pycnometer] I --> J[Refill with Distilled Water only
to calibration mark] J --> K[Weigh and Record W4
at test temperature T] K --> L[Compute Gs using Master Equation] L --> M[Apply Temperature Correction to 27 C if required] M --> N[Report Gs to two decimal places] ``` ### 4.2 Pycnometer Anatomy — Functional Cross-Section ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph P[Pycnometer Bottle Assembly] P1[Ground Glass Stopper
with Capillary Hole] --> P2[Calibration Mark on Neck] P2 --> P3[Bulb Body
500 mL nominal capacity] P3 --> P4[Flat Base for Stability] end S[Oven-Dry Soil Grains
Displacing water volume Vs] -. occupies .-> P3 W1[Distilled Water
Fills voids between grains] -. fills .-> P3 P1 -. allows excess water and air to escape .-> P2 ``` ### 4.3 The Four Weighings — Logical Information Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD O1[Stage 1: Empty Pycnometer
Weight equals W1] --> O2[Stage 2: Pycnometer plus Dry Soil
Weight equals W2] O2 --> O3[Stage 3: Pycnometer plus Soil plus Water to Mark
Weight equals W3] O3 --> O4[Stage 4: Pycnometer plus Water only to Mark
Weight equals W4] O1 --> CALC[Master Equation Application] O2 --> CALC O3 --> CALC O4 --> CALC CALC --> RESULT[Specific Gravity Gs at 27 C] ``` ### 4.4 Causes of Erroneous Gs — Diagnostic Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD Q{Gs is Outside Expected Range
2.60 to 2.80 for inorganic soils} -->|Low Gs below 2.50| R1[Check for trapped air
Increase vacuum time to 20 min] R1 --> R2[Check for organic content
Conduct loss on ignition test] Q -->|High Gs above 2.90| R3[Suspect heavy minerals
Magnetite, Hematite, Garnet] R3 --> R4[Verify with mineralogical analysis] Q -->|Erratic repeat readings| R5[Recheck balance calibration] R5 --> R6[Ensure consistent temperature T
Use water bath at 27 C] R6 --> R7[Re-weigh W4 immediately after W3
to prevent temperature drift] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1: Define Specific Gravity of Soil Solids. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Model Answer:** > **Specific Gravity of soil solids (Gs)** is defined as the ratio of the mass (or weight) of a given volume of soil solids to the mass (or weight) of an equal volume of distilled water at a standard temperature of **27 °C**, as per **IS 2720 (Part 3) – 1980**. > > $$G_s = \frac{W_s}{V_s \cdot \gamma_w}$$ > > It is a **dimensionless** quantity that depends only on the mineralogical composition of the soil grains. Typical values range from **2.65 for quartz-rich sands** to over **2.80 for clay-rich soils** containing iron oxides, and fall below **2.0 for organic/peat soils**. --- #### Question 2: Why is a vacuum pump (or gentle boiling) used during the pycnometer test? [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Model Answer:** > A vacuum pump or gentle boiling step is essential to **remove entrapped air bubbles** from the soil-water mixture inside the pycnometer. > > - Soil grains (especially fine-grained and angular particles) trap air in their inter-particle voids. > - If not removed, this air is counted as part of the soil volume, causing the measured volume $V_s$ to be **larger than the true solid volume**. > - Since $G_s = M_s / V_s \cdot \rho_w$, an inflated $V_s$ leads to a **falsely low** value of Gs. > - IS 2720 specifies a vacuum of approximately **700 mm Hg** applied for **20 minutes** for fine-grained soils, or **gentle boiling for 10 minutes** as an alternative. > - For **coarse-grained soils** (clean sand), a soaking period of **24 hours** is often sufficient. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently **interchange the order of operations** — for example, they compute the volume of water *after* the soil is added (which is fine) but **fail to subtract** the dry soil mass, producing a "specific gravity of the mixture" instead of the soil solids. Another common error is **forgetting the temperature correction** even when $T \neq 27$ °C, leading to a 0.01–0.02 error — enough to lose **2 marks** in ESE valuation. Always **state units**, **show the substitution step explicitly**, and **comment on the result's plausibility** at the end. #### Question A: Determination of Specific Gravity from Given Observations [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the procedure for determining the specific gravity of soil solids using a **pycnometer** as per IS 2720 (Part 3). State the formula and list the apparatus required. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** The following data were obtained in a pycnometer test on a soil sample: - $W_1$ (empty pycnometer) = **625.0 g** - $W_2$ (pycnometer + dry soil) = **1025.0 g** - $W_3$ (pycnometer + soil + water, full) = **1745.0 g** - $W_4$ (pycnometer + water only, full) = **1520.0 g** - Test temperature = **30 °C** Determine the specific gravity of the soil solids at 27 °C, and comment on the soil type. **[7 Marks]** --- ##### Model Solution for (a): **Apparatus Required:** > 1. Pycnometer (500 mL or 1000 mL) with a ground glass stopper and capillary hole > 2. Weighing balance (accuracy 0.01 g) > 3. Vacuum desiccator or vacuum pump > 4. Distilled water > 5. Thermometer (range 0–50 °C, accuracy 0.5 °C) > 6. Oven (105–110 °C for drying) > 7. Glass rod, wash bottle, towel **Procedure (as per IS 2720 Part 3):** 1. Clean the pycnometer thoroughly, dry it, and weigh it as **$W_1$**. 2. Take an **oven-dried soil sample** (about 200–400 g for sandy soil, 100–200 g for clayey soil). Place it in the pycnometer and weigh as **$W_2$**. 3. Add distilled water to cover the soil by about 25 mm and stir gently with a glass rod to dislodge entrapped air. 4. Apply **vacuum** for ~20 minutes (or **boil gently** for 10 minutes for coarse soils) to remove all entrapped air. 5. Cool the contents to room temperature; top up the pycnometer with distilled water exactly to the **calibration mark** on the capillary stopper. Wipe the outside dry and weigh as **$W_3$**. Record the **temperature $T$** of the contents. 6. Empty the pycnometer, rinse it, refill with distilled water only to the same mark, and weigh as **$W_4$** at the **same temperature $T$**. **Formula:** $$G_s = \frac{W_2 - W_1}{(W_2 - W_1) + (W_4 - W_3)}$$ --- ##### Model Solution for (b): **Step 1 — Mass of dry soil solids** $$M_s = W_2 - W_1 = 1025.0 - 625.0 = 400.0 \text{ g}$$ **Step 2 — Mass of water equal in volume to soil solids** $$M_{w,\text{displaced}} = (W_2 - W_1) + (W_4 - W_3) = 400.0 + (1520.0 - 1745.0) = 400.0 - 225.0 = 175.0 \text{ g}$$ **Step 3 — Specific gravity at test temperature T = 30 °C** $$G_{s,30} = \frac{400.0}{175.0} = 2.286$$ **Step 4 — Apply temperature correction to 27 °C** $$G_{s,27} = G_{s,30} \times \frac{\rho_{w,30}}{\rho_{w,27}} = 2.286 \times \frac{0.9956}{0.9965} = 2.286 \times 0.9991 = 2.284$$ **Step 5 — Final answer and interpretation** $$\boxed{\,G_{s,27} \approx 2.28\,}$$ **Comment:** A specific gravity of **2.28** is **lower than the typical range for inorganic mineral soils (2.65–2.80)**. This indicates either: - **Organic content** in the soil (most likely), - **Incomplete de-airing** during the test (underestimated displaced water mass), - Or a **calibration error** in the balance. A repeat test with longer vacuum application and possible Loss-on-Ignition test is recommended. --- #### Question B: Alternative Question — Significance and Phase Relation [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Discuss the **significance of specific gravity** in geotechnical engineering practice. Explain why the value differs between sandy, silty, and clayey soils, and how it is used in the computation of the **void ratio** and **degree of saturation**. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** In a pycnometer test, the following observations were recorded: - $W_1$ = **410.5 g** - $W_2$ = **710.5 g** - $W_3$ = **1465.0 g** - $W_4$ = **1290.0 g** - Test temperature $T$ = **20 °C** If the natural water content of the soil is **w = 12%** and the bulk density is **ρ = 1.95 g/cm³**, determine the **void ratio (e)**, **dry density (ρ_d)**, and **degree of saturation (S)**. **[7 Marks]** --- ##### Model Solution for (a): **Significance of Specific Gravity in Geotechnical Engineering:** Specific gravity ($G_s$) is a fundamental index property of soils with the following engineering applications: 1. **Phase Relationship Computation:** $G_s$ is required to convert measured bulk density and water content into derived quantities such as dry density ($\rho_d$), void ratio ($e$), and degree of saturation ($S$). 2. **Soil Identification:** $G_s$ acts as a fingerprint of soil mineralogy: - **Quartz (sand):** $G_s \approx 2.65$ - **Feldspar, mica:** $G_s \approx 2.60 - 2.80$ - **Iron-rich minerals (magnetite, hematite):** $G_s > 2.90$ - **Organic matter (humus, peat):** $G_s < 2.0$ (water ≈ 1.0) 3. **Indicator of Organic Content:** A sudden drop in Gs below 2.5 often signals organic contamination, which drastically affects compressibility and shear strength. 4. **Design of Earthworks:** Used in volume-change calculations for embankments, borrow pits, and compaction control. **Variation Among Soil Types:** | Soil Type | Dominant Mineral | Typical $G_s$ | |---|---|---| | Sand | Quartz (SiO₂) | 2.65 – 2.67 | | Silt | Quartz + Feldspar + Mica | 2.67 – 2.70 | | Clay | Kaolinite, Illite, Montmorillonite | 2.70 – 2.80 | | Organic | Carbon, humus | < 2.00 | **Role in Void Ratio and Degree of Saturation:** The void ratio is computed as: $$e = \frac{G_s \cdot \rho_w}{\rho_d} - 1$$ The degree of saturation uses the fundamental phase relation: $$S \cdot e = w \cdot G_s \quad\Longrightarrow\quad S = \frac{w \cdot G_s}{e}$$ Both equations **explicitly require $G_s$** as an input. --- ##### Model Solution for (b): **Step 1 — Compute Gs at 20 °C** $$M_s = W_2 - W_1 = 710.5 - 410.5 = 300.0 \text{ g}$$ $$M_{w,\text{displaced}} = (W_2 - W_1) + (W_4 - W_3) = 300.0 + (1290.0 - 1465.0) = 300.0 - 175.0 = 125.0 \text{ g}$$ $$G_{s,20} = \frac{300.0}{125.0} = 2.40$$ **Step 2 — Apply temperature correction to 27 °C** $$G_{s,27} = 2.40 \times \frac{\rho_{w,20}}{\rho_{w,27}} = 2.40 \times \frac{0.9982}{0.9965} = 2.40 \times 1.0017 = 2.404 \approx 2.40$$ **Step 3 — Compute dry density** $$\rho_d = \frac{\rho}{1 + w} = \frac{1.95}{1 + 0.12} = \frac{1.95}{1.12} = 1.741 \text{ g/cm}^3$$ **Step 4 — Compute void ratio** $$e = \frac{G_s \cdot \rho_w}{\rho_d} - 1 = \frac{2.40 \times 0.9965}{1.741} - 1 = \frac{2.392}{1.741} - 1 = 1.374 - 1 = 0.374$$ **Step 5 — Compute degree of saturation** $$S = \frac{w \cdot G_s}{e} = \frac{0.12 \times 2.40}{0.374} = \frac{0.288}{0.374} = 0.770 = 77.0\%$$ **Step 6 — Final results** $$\boxed{\,\rho_d = 1.74 \text{ g/cm}^3, \quad e = 0.374, \quad S = 77\%\,}$$ --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Quick-Recall Master List — Specific Gravity by Pycnometer** - ✅ **Definition:** $G_s = \dfrac{\text{Weight of soil solids}}{\text{Weight of equal volume of water at 27 °C}}$; **dimensionless**. - ✅ **IS Code:** IS 2720: Part 3 – 1980 (Reaffirmed 2002). - ✅ **Standard temperature for reporting:** **27 °C**. - ✅ **Master equation:** $G_s = \dfrac{W_2 - W_1}{(W_2 - W_1) + (W_4 - W_3)}$. - ✅ **Pycnometer size:** 500 mL (typical for fine soils); 1000 mL for coarse soils. - ✅ **Sample mass:** ~100 g (clay) to ~400 g (sand). - ✅ **De-airing:** Vacuum (~700 mm Hg) for **20 min** OR gentle boiling for **10 min** — mandatory to avoid low Gs. - ✅ **Oven-dry temperature:** **105–110 °C** for 16–24 hours. - ✅ **Order of weighings:** $W_1 \to W_2 \to W_3 \to W_4$ — never reverse; $W_4$ is the calibration. - ✅ **Temperature correction:** Use $\rho_w$ tables; ratio is $\rho_{w,T} / \rho_{w,27}$. - ✅ **Typical Gs values:** Sand **2.65–2.67**; Silt **2.67–2.70**; Clay **2.70–2.80**; Organic **< 2.0**. - ✅ **Phase relations dependent on Gs:** $\rho_d$, $e$, $S$, $n$, $\gamma_d$, $\gamma_{\text{sat}}$. - ✅ **Common error to avoid:** Confusing Gs (soil solids only) with bulk specific gravity (whole sample including voids). - ✅ **Default assumed value:** $G_s = 2.65$ for quartzitic sand when testing is not performed. - ✅ **Repeatability criterion:** Two consecutive values should agree within **± 0.02** for a valid test. - ✅ **Examiner's pet trick:** Always ask "What if the soil contains soluble salts?" → Use **kerosene** instead of water (with appropriate correction) to prevent salt dissolution from altering the apparent Gs.

topics.title: Determination of Water Content - Oven Drying Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL408 - GEOTECHNICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Water Content - Oven Drying Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Water Content (w)**: The ratio of the mass of water ($M_w$) present in the voids of a soil mass to the mass of the solid particles ($M_s$) of that soil, expressed as a percentage. It is one of the most fundamental index properties of soil and is governed by the **Indian Standard IS 2720 (Part II) – 1973** (Reaffirmed 2020) under the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) for laboratory determination.

The **Oven Drying Method** is the standard, most accurate, and most widely accepted laboratory method for determining the natural water content of soil. The principle is based on the measurement of the **loss in mass** of a wet soil specimen when it is subjected to controlled drying in a thermostatically regulated hot-air oven.

Mathematically, the basic governing relationship is:

$$w = \frac{M_w}{M_s} \times 100\ \%$$

where:
- $w$ = Water content of the soil (%)
- $M_w$ = Mass of water present in the soil voids
- $M_s$ = Mass of dry soil solids (oven-dried mass)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy – "The Tea Cup Test"**:
> Imagine you have a tea cup with wet tea leaves stuck to the inside walls. To find out how much water is actually in those leaves, you could weigh the cup, leaves, and water together (let's call this the "wet weight"). Then you could place the cup in a hot oven to evaporate all the water, let it cool, and weigh it again (the "dry weight"). The difference between the two weights is the weight of water that escaped as steam.
>
> In soil mechanics, we do the exact same thing — except our "tea cup" is a corrosion-resistant **moisture can**, our "tea leaves" are soil particles, and our "steam" is the pore water that we want to quantify. The oven acts as a deterministic, repeatable "evaporator" that ensures we remove **only the water** and not destroy the soil minerals.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Specifications

- **Standard Drying Temperature:** **105 °C to 110 °C** (for inorganic soils, clays, sands, gravels).
- **Special Temperature (Organic & Gypsiferous Soils):** **60 °C to 80 °C** — to prevent burning of organic matter and loss of crystalline water from gypsum.
- **Duration of Drying:** **16 to 24 hours** (overnight cycle is standard lab practice).
- **Balance Accuracy:** **0.01 g** (for specimens ≤ 200 g) and **0.1 g** (for specimens > 200 g).
- **IS Code Reference:** **IS 2720 (Part II) – 1973**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight – Why this Experiment?**
> Water content is the **gateway index property**. It directly influences consistency limits (Liquid Limit, Plastic Limit), compaction characteristics, shear strength, and effective stress. Every subsequent geotechnical design — from a small residential footing to a massive earthen dam — eventually traces back to the accuracy of this one measurement. KTU examiners frequently frame this as a "core experiment" worth 14 marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Principle – The "Why" Behind the Method

The Oven Drying Method works on the principle of **gravimetric mass loss** under controlled thermal conditions:

1. A wet soil specimen of known mass is exposed to a hot-air convection environment at **105 °C – 110 °C**.
2. At this temperature, the **free (pore) water** within the soil voids evaporates completely.
3. The temperature is deliberately kept **below 110 °C** to ensure that:
   - **Structural water** (chemically bound water in clay minerals like kaolinite, montmorillonite) is **not** driven off.
   - **Organic matter** in the soil is **not** oxidized or burnt.
   - **Volatile salts** do not decompose.
4. After drying, the mass of the soil represents **only the solid particles** ($M_s$).
5. The difference between the original wet mass and the oven-dry mass is the mass of water ($M_w$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Not Use a Microwave or Stove?** — Open-flame or microwave heating causes localized overheating (>200 °C), which destroys organic matter, dehydrates clay minerals, and gives a falsely high water content. The controlled laboratory oven ensures a uniform, reproducible, and scientifically valid measurement.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table verbatim — KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation) problems directly substitute numerical values into these formulas.

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Water Content (Fundamental) | $w$ | $w = \dfrac{M_w}{M_s} \times 100$ | % | Master equation |
| Mass of Empty Container + Lid | $M_1$ | Measured directly | grams (g) | Weighed at start |
| Mass of Container + Wet Soil + Lid | $M_2$ | Measured directly | grams (g) | Weighed before oven |
| Mass of Container + Dry Soil + Lid | $M_3$ | Measured after oven cooling | grams (g) | Weighed after oven |
| Mass of Water | $M_w$ | $M_w = M_2 - M_3$ | grams (g) | Loss in mass |
| Mass of Dry Soil | $M_s$ | $M_s = M_3 - M_1$ | grams (g) | Solid particles only |
| **Working Formula for Oven Method** | $w$ | $w = \dfrac{M_2 - M_3}{M_3 - M_1} \times 100$ | % | **Most used in KTU exams** |
| Constant Mass Criterion | $\Delta M$ | $M_3' - M_3 \leq 0.1\% \text{ of } M_3$ | grams (g) | Repeat till achieved |
| Minimum Specimen Mass (per IS 2720 Pt. II) | $m_{min}$ | Varies with particle size | grams (g) | See table below |

### Minimum Specimen Mass Table (KTU Frequently Asked)

| **Soil Type / Particle Size** | **Minimum Wet Specimen Mass** |
|---|---|
| Clay, Silt, Fine Sand (particles < 2 mm) | **30 g** (use 50–100 g for higher accuracy) |
| Sandy Soil (particles < 10 mm) | **300 g** |
| Gravelly Soil (particles < 20 mm) | **1000 g** |
| Coarse Gravel / Stony Soil (particles < 40 mm) | **3000 g** |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Earthwork Construction (Embankments, Dams, Highways):** Field engineers must verify that the soil being compacted has the **optimum moisture content (OMC)** required for maximum dry density.
- **Slope Stability & Landslide Analysis:** Pore water pressure (and hence water content) directly governs the factor of safety of natural and man-made slopes.
- **Bearing Capacity of Foundations:** Bearing capacity equations (Terzaghi, Meyerhof, IS 6403) all use saturated vs. unsaturated shear strength parameters derived from water content data.
- **Shrinkage and Swell Behaviour:** Expansive clay soils (like black cotton soil) change volume with water content, damaging lightweight structures.
- **Soil Stabilization Design:** The quantity of lime, cement, or bitumen to be added is decided based on the in-situ water content.

## 2.4 Apparatus & Materials (KTU Practical Viva Standard List)

1. **Hot-Air Oven** — Thermostatically controlled, capable of maintaining **105 °C – 110 °C**, with internal ventilation.
2. **Moisture Cans** (also called "moisture boxes" or "evaporating dishes") — corrosion-resistant (aluminium or stainless steel), with tightly fitting lids.
3. **Electronic/Physical Weighing Balance** — readability **0.01 g**.
4. **Desiccator** — airtight glass/plastic chamber containing anhydrous **silica gel** or **calcium chloride** desiccant, used for gradual cooling.
5. **Tongs** — for handling hot moisture cans (NEVER use bare hands; oil from skin alters mass).
6. **Spatula / Palette Knife** — for transferring soil.
7. **Sample Splitter / Quartering Tool** — for obtaining a **representative specimen**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP PROCEDURE & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Detailed Step-by-Step Laboratory Procedure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** Examiners award marks for *logical sequencing* and *mentioning justifications*. Do not just list steps — explain **why** each step is performed.

### **Step 1 — Preparation of Moisture Cans**
- Clean the empty moisture cans and their lids thoroughly.
- Wipe them dry and place them (with lids kept slightly open or beside them) in the oven for **at least 30 minutes** at 105 °C to remove any residual moisture.
- Remove, cool in the desiccator for **15–20 minutes**, and weigh to the nearest **0.01 g**.
- **Record this mass as $M_1$** (mass of empty container + lid).

*Justification:* Even trace moisture in the can would cause a false reading of dry soil mass.

### **Step 2 — Obtaining a Representative Soil Specimen**
- Use the **quartering or riffle-box sampling** method to obtain a representative portion from the bulk soil sample.
- The minimum mass required depends on the maximum particle size (refer to the table in Section 2.2).
- **For fine-grained soils (typical KTU exam context):** Take approximately **30 g to 50 g** of wet soil.

*Justification:* A non-representative sample introduces sampling error — the most common source of inaccuracy in this experiment.

### **Step 3 — Weighing the Wet Soil + Container**
- Place the wet soil specimen into the pre-weighed moisture can.
- **Immediately** cover it with the lid to prevent evaporation of moisture into the surrounding air (which would artificially lower the measured water content).
- Weigh to the nearest **0.01 g**.
- **Record this mass as $M_2$** (mass of container + lid + wet soil).

*Justification:* Speed matters — every second the lid is off, water evaporates. This is the "race against the clock" principle in water content determination.

### **Step 4 — Oven Drying**
- Remove the lid and place it underneath or beside the can (do not throw it away — it must be reweighed with the can).
- Place the can with the wet soil in the **thermostatically controlled oven** maintained at **105 °C – 110 °C**.
- Allow it to dry for **16 to 24 hours** (typically overnight).

*Justification:* This duration ensures complete evaporation of free pore water, even from the deepest interior of the specimen.

### **Step 5 — Cooling in Desiccator**
- After drying, remove the can from the oven using **tongs** (NOT bare hands — the oils, salts, and moisture from your skin will alter the mass).
- Cover the can with the lid immediately.
- Place the covered can in the **desiccator** to cool gradually in a moisture-free environment for **15 to 30 minutes** (until it reaches room temperature).

*Justification:* Hot cans placed directly on a balance create convection currents that give erratic readings. Hot cans exposed to ambient air absorb moisture rapidly, giving a falsely high $M_3$.

### **Step 6 — Weighing the Dry Soil + Container (First Iteration)**
- Weigh the cooled, dry soil + container + lid to the nearest 0.01 g.
- **Record this mass as $M_3$** (intermediate dry mass).

### **Step 7 — Verification of Constant Mass**
- Reintroduce the can (with lid off) into the oven for an **additional 1 to 2 hours** of drying.
- Cool again in the desiccator and reweigh.
- **Record this mass as $M_3'$** (final dry mass).
- **Criterion for completion:** $M_3' - M_3 \leq 0.1\% \text{ of } M_3$.

*Justification:* A decreasing mass after re-drying means water is still being lost. The experiment is complete only when mass becomes constant.

### **Step 8 — Calculation and Reporting**
- Apply the working formula to compute $w$.
- Report water content to **two decimal places** (e.g., $w = 18.45\%$).

## 3.2 Exhaustive Sample Calculation (Typical KTU 2024 Numerical)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> In a water content determination test using the oven drying method, the following observations were recorded. Calculate the water content of the soil specimen.

**Given Data:**

| **Observation** | **Value (g)** |
|---|---|
| Mass of empty container with lid ($M_1$) | 24.50 |
| Mass of container + wet soil + lid ($M_2$) | 78.65 |
| Mass of container + dry soil + lid, 1st weighing ($M_3$) | 70.78 |
| Mass of container + dry soil + lid, 2nd weighing ($M_3'$) | 70.76 |

### **Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute Mass of Water ($M_w$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_w &= M_2 - M_3 \\
&= 78.65 \text{ g} - 70.78 \text{ g} \\
&= 7.87 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* The mass lost between the wet stage ($M_2$) and the dry stage ($M_3$) represents the water that evaporated in the oven.

**Step 2:** Compute Mass of Dry Soil Solids ($M_s$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_s &= M_3 - M_1 \\
&= 70.78 \text{ g} - 24.50 \text{ g} \\
&= 46.28 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Subtracting the empty container mass from the dry mass gives the mass of the soil solids only.

**Step 3:** Check Constant Mass Criterion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta M &= M_3' - M_3 = 70.76 - 70.78 = -0.02 \text{ g} \\
|\Delta M| &= 0.02 \text{ g} \\
0.1\% \text{ of } M_3 &= \frac{0.1}{100} \times 70.78 = 0.07078 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0.02 \text{ g} < 0.07078 \text{ g}$, the constant mass criterion is satisfied. The drying is **complete**.

**Step 4:** Apply the Working Formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w &= \frac{M_2 - M_3}{M_3 - M_1} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{7.87}{46.28} \times 100 \\
&= 17.005... \ \% \\
&\approx 17.01 \ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The water content of the soil specimen is $\boxed{w = 17.01\ \%}$.

## 3.3 Sources of Error & Precautions (Analytical Matrix)

| **Error / Mistake** | **Impact on Result** | **Precaution / Correction** |
|---|---|---|
| Hot can weighed directly | Convection gives erratic mass; mass reading fluctuates | Always cool in desiccator before weighing |
| Lid left off wet sample | Water evaporates, $M_2$ is falsely low → $w$ under-reported | Cover can with lid immediately after sampling |
| Hands used to handle hot can | Oil + sweat transferred to can → mass increases | Always use tongs |
| Insufficient drying time | Water remains → $M_3$ is high → $w$ under-reported | Dry for ≥ 16 hours and verify with constant mass test |
| Organic soil dried at 110 °C | Organic matter burns off → $M_3$ is too low → $w$ over-reported | Dry organic/gypsiferous soils at 60 °C – 80 °C |
| Non-representative sampling | Sample does not represent bulk soil | Use quartering or riffle-box method |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Oven Drying Method (Mermaid)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid flowchart represents the complete logical flow of the water content determination experiment, from sample collection to final calculation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Collect Representative Soil Sample"] --> B["Clean Dry and Weigh Empty Moisture Can with Lid M1"]
    B --> C["Place Wet Soil into Moisture Can"]
    C --> D["Cover with Lid Immediately and Weigh M2"]
    D --> E["Remove Lid and Place Can in Oven at 105 to 110 Degree Celsius"]
    E --> F["Dry for 16 to 24 Hours"]
    F --> G["Remove Can with Tongs and Cover with Lid"]
    G --> H["Cool in Desiccator for 15 to 30 Minutes"]
    H --> I["Weigh Dry Soil Plus Container Plus Lid as M3"]
    I --> J{"Is Constant Mass Achieved Difference less than 0.1 percent of M3"}
    J -- "No" --> K["Return to Oven for Additional 1 to 2 Hours"]
    K --> H
    J -- "Yes" --> L["Calculate Mass of Water Mw equals M2 minus M3"]
    L --> M["Calculate Mass of Dry Soil Ms equals M3 minus M1"]
    M --> N["Compute Water Content w equals Mw by Ms times 100"]
    N --> O["Report w in Percentage to Two Decimal Places"]
    O --> P["End of Experiment"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Apparatus Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputSubsystem ["INPUT SUBSYSTEM"]
        A1["Representative Soil Specimen"]
        A2["Pre-weighed Moisture Can with Lid"]
    end

    subgraph DryingSubsystem ["DRYING SUBSYSTEM"]
        B1["Hot-Air Oven 105 to 110 Degree Celsius"]
        B2["Thermostat Temperature Controller"]
        B3["Internal Convection Fan"]
    end

    subgraph CoolingSubsystem ["COOLING SUBSYSTEM"]
        C1["Desiccator with Silica Gel"]
        C2["Tongs for Hot Can Handling"]
    end

    subgraph MeasurementSubsystem ["MEASUREMENT SUBSYSTEM"]
        D1["Electronic Balance 0.01 g Accuracy"]
        D2["Observation Table M1, M2, M3"]
    end

    subgraph OutputSubsystem ["OUTPUT SUBSYSTEM"]
        E1["Water Content w in percent"]
        E2["Final Report to Two Decimal Places"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B2 -.controls.-> B1
    B3 -.assists.-> B1
    B1 --> C1
    C2 --> C1
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic Diagram — Which Drying Temperature to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Identify Soil Type from Visual and Olfactory Inspection"] --> B{"Does Soil Contain Organic Matter or Gypsum"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Use Low Temperature 60 to 80 Degree Celsius"]
    B -- "No" --> D{"Is Soil Inorganic Clay Silt Sand or Gravel"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["Use Standard Temperature 105 to 110 Degree Celsius"]
    D -- "Unknown" --> F["Conduct Trial Drying and Observe Mass Loss Trend"]
    F --> E
    C --> G["Proceed with Drying and Weighing Cycle"]
    E --> G
    G --> H["Compute Water Content and Report"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define water content. List any four methods of determining the water content of soil in the laboratory.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Water Content (w):** It is defined as the ratio of the mass of water ($M_w$) present in the voids of a soil mass to the mass of the solid particles ($M_s$) of that soil, expressed as a percentage.
>
> **Formula:** $w = \dfrac{M_w}{M_s} \times 100\ \%$
>
> **Four Laboratory Methods of Water Content Determination:**
>
> 1. **Oven Drying Method** (IS 2720 Part II) — Standard reference method using 105–110 °C.
> 2. **Sand Bath Method** — A quick field-adjacent method where soil is dried over a sand bath heated by a stove.
> 3. **Calcium Carbide Method (Speedy Moisture Meter)** — Water reacts with calcium carbide to produce acetylene gas; pressure is correlated to water content.
> 4. **Torsion Balance Method (Infrared Moisture Meter)** — Uses an infrared lamp for drying and a torsion balance for continuous mass measurement.
> 5. **Alcohol Method** — Soil is mixed with methylated spirit and ignited; the heat of combustion evaporates pore water.
> 6. **Pycnometer Method** — Used for cohesionless soils; based on volume displacement.

*(Any four of the above suffice for full marks.)*

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Why is the standard drying temperature for the oven drying method kept between 105 °C and 110 °C and not higher? Mention the special case.**

**Model Answer:**

The drying temperature is kept between **105 °C and 110 °C** for the following reasons:

1. At this range, **all free (pore) water** in the soil voids evaporates completely.
2. The temperature is **insufficient to drive off the structural (chemically bound) water** held within the clay mineral lattices (e.g., kaolinite, montmorillonite, illite). Exceeding this range would artificially increase the apparent water content.
3. The temperature is **low enough to prevent the combustion or oxidation of organic matter** present in the soil, which would otherwise cause mass loss not attributable to water.

> **Special Case:** For soils containing **organic matter, gypsum, or other volatile salts**, a **lower temperature of 60 °C to 80 °C** is used to prevent the loss of organic content and the escape of crystalline water from gypsum.

---

## 5.2 PART B — ESE Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** In Part B, Module Internal Choice is mandatory. Students must answer either Question A OR Question B in full. Each question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks** sub-parts.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **oven drying method** for the determination of water content of soil as per **IS 2720 (Part II) – 1973**. Include the apparatus list, procedure, formula, and the significance of the constant mass test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data were recorded during a water content determination test. Calculate the water content and verify whether the constant mass criterion has been satisfied. **(7 marks)**

| **Observation** | **Value (g)** |
|---|---|
| Mass of empty moisture can with lid ($M_1$) | 32.45 |
| Mass of can + wet soil + lid ($M_2$) | 96.80 |
| Mass of can + dry soil + lid, 1st reading ($M_3$) | 87.62 |
| Mass of can + dry soil + lid, 2nd reading ($M_3'$) | 87.58 |

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 marks**

**Apparatus Required (1 mark):**
- Thermostatically controlled hot-air oven (105 °C – 110 °C)
- Moisture cans (corrosion-resistant) with lids
- Electronic weighing balance (accuracy 0.01 g)
- Desiccator with silica gel desiccant
- Tongs, spatula, sample splitter

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. Clean, dry, and weigh the empty moisture can with lid ($M_1$).
2. Place a representative wet soil specimen (≥ 30 g for fine soils) into the can; cover with lid immediately and weigh ($M_2$).
3. Place the can (lid removed) in the oven at 105–110 °C for 16–24 hours.
4. Remove with tongs, cover with lid, and cool in the desiccator for 15–30 minutes.
5. Weigh to obtain $M_3$ (first dry mass).
6. Re-dry for 1–2 hours, cool, and reweigh to obtain $M_3'$.

**Constant Mass Test (1 mark):**
The drying is considered complete when the difference between successive dry mass readings is less than **0.1% of $M_3$**, i.e., $M_3 - M_3' \leq 0.001 \times M_3$. This ensures that all free water has been driven off.

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$
w = \frac{M_2 - M_3}{M_3 - M_1} \times 100\ \%
$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Mass of water:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_w &= M_2 - M_3 \\
&= 96.80 - 87.62 \\
&= 9.18 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Mass of dry soil:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_s &= M_3 - M_1 \\
&= 87.62 - 32.45 \\
&= 55.17 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Constant mass verification:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta M &= M_3 - M_3' = 87.62 - 87.58 = 0.04 \text{ g} \\
\text{Allowable limit} &= 0.1\% \text{ of } M_3 = \frac{0.1}{100} \times 87.62 = 0.08762 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\Delta M = 0.04 \text{ g} < 0.0876 \text{ g}$, the constant mass criterion is **satisfied**.

**Step 4:** Water content calculation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w &= \frac{M_w}{M_s} \times 100 = \frac{9.18}{55.17} \times 100 \\
&= 16.6394 \ \% \\
&\approx 16.64 \ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{w = 16.64\ \%}$ and the constant mass criterion is satisfied. ✅

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Discuss the **apparatus, principle, procedure, and precautions** to be observed during the determination of water content by the oven drying method. Why is it considered the most accurate method? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wet soil specimen weighing **425 g** has a water content of **18%**. The same soil is dried in an oven. Find:
  (i) Mass of water in the original specimen
  (ii) Mass of dry soil
  (iii) If the dry specimen is later re-wetted by adding **30 g of water**, determine the new water content. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 marks**

**Principle (2 marks):**
The oven drying method is based on the principle of **gravimetric mass loss**. A wet soil sample is dried in a hot-air oven at **105 °C – 110 °C** until all free pore water evaporates. The difference between the wet mass and the oven-dry mass represents the mass of water originally present in the voids.

**Apparatus and Procedure (3 marks):**
Refer to Sections 2.4 and 3.1 of these notes for a complete listing. The procedure involves weighing the empty can ($M_1$), the wet soil + can ($M_2$), and the dry soil + can ($M_3$) using an electronic balance with 0.01 g accuracy, with desiccator cooling between weighings.

**Precautions (1 mark):**
- Use tongs, not bare hands, to handle hot cans.
- Cover the wet sample immediately to prevent evaporation losses.
- Cool in a desiccator before weighing.
- Verify constant mass before terminating the test.
- Use the lower temperature (60–80 °C) for organic and gypsiferous soils.

**Why Most Accurate? (1 mark):**
The oven drying method is considered the most accurate because:
- It uses a **precisely controlled temperature** (105–110 °C) that is high enough to remove all free water but low enough to avoid decomposition of organic matter and loss of structural water.
- The mass measurements are made using a high-precision **0.01 g balance**.
- It is the **standard reference method** (IS 2720 Part II) against which all other methods are calibrated.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** Wet soil mass $M_{wet} = 425$ g, water content $w_1 = 18\% = 0.18$.

**Step (i): Mass of water in the original specimen (2 marks)**

By definition:

$$
w = \frac{M_w}{M_s} \implies M_w = w \times M_s
$$

Also, $M_{wet} = M_w + M_s = 425$ g. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_w &= w \times M_s = 0.18 \times M_s \\
M_w + M_s &= 425 \\
0.18 M_s + M_s &= 425 \\
1.18 M_s &= 425 \\
M_s &= \frac{425}{1.18} = 360.17 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute $M_w$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_w &= 425 - 360.17 = 64.83 \text{ g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mass of water = 64.83 g** ✅

**Step (ii): Mass of dry soil (2 marks)**

From the calculation above:

$$
M_s = 360.17 \text{ g} \quad \text{(mass of dry soil)} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step (iii): New water content after adding 30 g of water (3 marks)**

The mass of dry soil remains the same ($M_s = 360.17$ g) since drying does not change the solid mass.
The new mass of water is:

$$
M_{w,\text{new}} = 64.83 + 30 = 94.83 \text{ g}
$$

The new water content is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_2 &= \frac{M_{w,\text{new}}}{M_s} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{94.83}{360.17} \times 100 \\
&= 26.330 \ \% \\
&\approx 26.33 \ \%
\end{aligned}
$$
