### 4.2 Block Diagram — Casagrande Liquid Limit Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[START: Air-dry 150 g soil pass 425 micron IS sieve] --> S2[Mix with distilled water to thick paste]
    S2 --> S3[Place paste in Casagrande cup, level surface, max depth 10 mm]
    S3 --> S4[Cut V-groove with standard grooving tool through centre]
    S4 --> S5[Lift and drop cup at 2 revolutions per second]
    S5 --> S6{Groove closed by 12.7 mm?}
    S6 -- No --> S7[Add more water, remix, repeat steps 3 to 6]
    S6 -- Yes --> S8[Record number of blows N]
    S8 --> S9[Take moisture sample from cup immediately, weigh, oven-dry 24 hr]
    S9 --> S10[Compute water content w for this trial]
    S10 --> S11{Number of blows between 15 and 35?}
    S11 -- No --> S2
    S11 -- Yes --> S12[Repeat for 3 to 4 different water contents]
    S12 --> S13[Plot Flow Curve: w vs log10 N]
    S13 --> S14[Read W_L at N equals 25]

    style S1 fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:2px
    style S14 fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#b7950b,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Shrinkage Limit Test (Mercury Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Weigh empty shrinkage dish M_3] --> A2[Fill dish with saturated soil paste, weigh M_1]
    A2 --> A3[Air-dry then oven-dry 24 hr at 105 to 110 deg C]
    A3 --> A4[Weigh oven-dry pat plus dish M_2]
    A4 --> A5[Compute initial water content w]
    A4 --> A6[Suspend dry pat with wire loop, immerse in mercury dish]
    A6 --> A7[Weigh displaced mercury mass M_Hg]
    A7 --> A8[Compute dry volume V_d equals M_Hg divided by 13.6]
    A2 --> A9[Measure wet volume V_1 by filling dish brimful with water or mercury]
    A9 --> A10[Compute Shrinkage Ratio R_S]
    A10 --> A11[Apply formula W_S equals w minus R_S times 1 minus V_d over V_1]
    A11 --> A12[REPORT W_S to 2 decimal places in percent]

    style A12 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px
    style A11 fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#b7950b,stroke-width:2px
    style A1 fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Plastic Limit Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Take 20 g of air-dry soil] --> P2[Mix with distilled water to plastic consistency]
    P2 --> P3[Roll between palm and glass plate to form thread]
    P3 --> P4{Thread diameter reached 3 mm?}
    P4 -- No --> P3
    P4 -- Yes --> P5{Thread crumbling with transverse cracks?}
    P5 -- No --> P3
    P5 -- Yes --> P6[Collect crumbled thread, weigh in moisture can]
    P6 --> P7[Oven-dry 24 hr, reweigh]
    P7 --> P8[Compute water content w of this thread]
    P8 --> P9{Three trials completed?}
    P9 -- No --> P2
    P9 -- Yes --> P10{Spread of three values within 2 percent?}
    P10 -- No --> P11[REPEAT entire test with fresh sample]
    P10 -- Yes --> P12[REPORT mean of three values as W_P]

    style P12 fill:#abebc6,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px
    style P11 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between the **Liquid Limit** and **Plastic Limit** of a fine-grained soil. State the IS code and apparatus used for each test.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Liquid Limit ($W_L$) | Plastic Limit ($W_P$) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Min. water content at which soil begins to flow under a small shearing stress | Min. water content at which soil can be rolled into a 3 mm thread without crumbling |
| IS Code | IS 2720 (Part 5) | IS 2720 (Part 5) |
| Apparatus | Casagrande cup **OR** Cone penetrometer | Glass plate + hand rolling |
| Result interpretation | $W_L$ = water content at $N = 25$ blows | $W_P$ = water content at the moment the 3 mm thread crumbles |

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the **Plasticity Index**? Why is it considered the most important Atterberg property for classifying fine-grained soils?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Plasticity Index ($I_P$)** is the numerical difference between the Liquid Limit and Plastic Limit:

$$I_P = W_L - W_P \quad (\%) \quad \quad \text{...(1 mark for formula)}$$

It quantifies the **range of water content** over which the soil remains in a **plastic (mouldable) state** — i.e., how "clay-like" the soil is. **(1 mark)**

It is the most important Atterberg property because:
1. It is the primary input to the **Casagrande Plasticity Chart** (along with $W_L$) for soil classification as CL, CH, CI, ML, MH, or MI under **IS 1498 – 1970**. **(1 mark for application)**
2. A high $I_P$ indicates a highly plastic, potentially expansive clay (e.g., black cotton soil), which has severe **shrink-swell** implications for foundations.
3. It directly controls the **cohesion and undrained strength** of a remoulded clay mass.

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note for Students (KTU 2024 Scheme):** In the End Semester Exam (ESE), Module 2 typically features an **internal choice** — i.e., **Q9(a) OR Q9(b)**. Each carries 14 marks split as **(a) = 7 marks, (b) = 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** The following observations were recorded during a **Casagrande Liquid Limit** test on a fine-grained soil sample passing the 425 µm IS sieve:

| Trial | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Number of blows, $N$ | 12 | 18 | 28 | 40 |
| Water content, $w$ (%) | 64.0 | 60.5 | 56.0 | 53.0 |

Plot the **flow curve** and determine the **Liquid Limit** and **Flow Index**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Tabulate $\log_{10} N$:**

$$\log_{10}(12) = 1.079, \quad \log_{10}(18) = 1.255, \quad \log_{10}(28) = 1.447, \quad \log_{10}(40) = 1.602$$

| $N$ | 12 | 18 | 28 | 40 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\log_{10} N$ | 1.079 | 1.255 | 1.447 | 1.602 |
| $w$ (%) | 64.0 | 60.5 | 56.0 | 53.0 |

**Step 2 — Best-fit line (least-squares / graphical):**
Using endpoints (12, 64.0) and (40, 53.0):

$$T_f = \dfrac{64.0 - 53.0}{1.602 - 1.079} = \dfrac{11.0}{0.523} = 21.03$$

**Step 3 — Compute $W_L$ at $N = 25$ (so $\log_{10} 25 = 1.398$):**

Using trial 1 as reference:

$$W_L = 64.0 + 21.03 \times (1.398 - 1.079) = 64.0 + 21.03 \times 0.319 = 64.0 + 6.71 = 70.71\%$$

$$\boxed{W_L \approx 70.7\%} \quad \quad \text{and} \quad \boxed{T_f = 21.0\% \; \text{per log cycle}} \quad \text{(7 marks)}$

> **Awarding marks:** 1 mark for table, 1 mark for $\log_{10}$ conversion, 2 marks for $T_f$ calculation, 2 marks for $W_L$ at $N=25$, 1 mark for final result.

---

**(b)** A clay sample has the following properties:
- Liquid Limit, $W_L$ = **52%**
- Plastic Limit, $W_P$ = **24%**
- In-situ water content, $w$ = **32%**
- Shrinkage Limit, $W_S$ = **15%**

Compute the **Plasticity Index ($I_P$)**, **Consistency Index ($I_C$)**, **Liquidity Index ($I_L$)**, and **Shrinkage Index ($I_S$)**. Classify the soil's consistency state. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Plasticity Index:**

$$I_P = W_L - W_P = 52 - 24 = \mathbf{28\%} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Consistency Index:**

$$I_C = \dfrac{W_L - w}{I_P} = \dfrac{52 - 32}{28} = \dfrac{20}{28} = \mathbf{0.714} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 3 — Liquidity Index:**

$$I_L = \dfrac{w - W_P}{I_P} = \dfrac{32 - 24}{28} = \dfrac{8}{28} = \mathbf{0.286} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 4 — Shrinkage Index:**

$$I_S = W_P - W_S = 24 - 15 = \mathbf{9\%} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 5 — Consistency State Classification:**

Since $0 < I_C < 1$ (specifically $I_C = 0.714$) **and** $W_P < w < W_L$ (i.e., $24 < 32 < 52$), the soil is in the **PLASTIC STATE**, leaning toward the **stiff plastic** side. Because $I_L = 0.286 < 0.5$, the soil is **medium-plastic / firm plastic**, with no risk of undrained flow. **(2 marks)**

> **Quick check:** $I_C + I_L = 0.714 + 0.286 = 1.000$ ✓ (this identity always holds for consistent data.)

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Describe, with a neat labelled sketch, the **Casagrande Liquid Limit apparatus** and the procedure for determining the Liquid Limit. State two **precautions** to be observed during the test. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Apparatus description (3 marks):**
The Casagrande apparatus consists of:
- A **brass cup** of internal radius 54 mm, height 27 mm, mounted on a rigid bracket such that it can be lifted and dropped onto a **hard rubber base** of standard hardness (Shore A 80–90).
- A **standard grooving tool** (Casagrande or ASTM type) with dimensions specified by IS 2720 (Part 5).
- A **blow counter** and a **height adjustment screw** ensuring the cup drops exactly **10 mm** each time.
- A **caml** rotating at **2 revolutions per second (120 rpm)**.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Take ~120 g of air-dry soil passing 425 µm sieve, mix with distilled water to form a uniform thick paste.
2. Place the paste in the cup, level it parallel to the base (max depth 10 mm at the deepest point).
3. Cut a **V-shaped groove** down the centre using the grooving tool, holding it perpendicular to the cup surface.
4. Rotate the cam at **2 rev/s** and count the blows $N$ required for the **bottom of the groove to close by 12.7 mm**.
5. Immediately take a moisture sample from the closed groove zone, oven-dry for 24 h, compute $w$.
6. Repeat for **3–4 trials at different water contents**, aiming for $N$ values between **15 and 35**.
7. Plot $w$ vs. $\log_{10} N$, read $W_L$ at $N = 25$.

**Precautions (1 mark — any two of the following):**
- Always rotate the cam at the **standard 2 rev/s** speed.
- The groove must be cut in a **single clean stroke** — repeated scraping disturbs the soil structure.
- The cup must be **cleaned and dried** between trials; residue changes the drop height.
- Air-dry the soil **before mixing**; an over-wet sample gives inconsistent readings.

---

**(b)** In a **Shrinkage Limit test** (mercury displacement method) on a clay sample, the following data were recorded:
- Mass of empty shrinkage dish, $M_3$ = **32.6 g**
- Mass of dish + wet soil, $M_1$ = **82.4 g**
- Mass of dish + oven-dry soil, $M_2$ = **64.8 g**
- Mass of mercury displaced by the dry pat, $M_{Hg}$ = **183.5 g**
- Volume of wet soil pat (by water-filling), $V_1$ = **32.5 cm³**

Compute the **Shrinkage Limit**, **Shrinkage Ratio**, and **Volumetric Shrinkage**. Take $\rho_{Hg} = 13.6 \text{ g/cm}^3$ and $\rho_w = 1.0 \text{ g/cm}^3$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Dry soil mass:**

$$M_s = M_2 - M_3 = 64.8 - 32.6 = 32.2 \text{ g} \quad \text{(0.5 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Initial water content:**

$$w = \dfrac{M_1 - M_2}{M_s} \times 100 = \dfrac{82.4 - 64.8}{32.2} \times 100 = \dfrac{17.6}{32.2} \times 100 = 54.66\% \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Volume of dry pat:**

$$V_d = \dfrac{M_{Hg}}{\rho_{Hg}} = \dfrac{183.5}{13.6} = 13.49 \text{ cm}^3 \quad \text{(0.5 mark)}$$

**Step 4 — Shrinkage Ratio:**

$$R_S = \dfrac{V_1 - V_d}{M_s} = \dfrac{32.5 - 13.49}{32.2} = \dfrac{19.01}{32.2} = 0.5904 \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 5 — Shrinkage Limit:**

$$W_S = w - R_S \times \left(1 - \dfrac{V_d}{V_1}\right) = 54.66 - 0.5904 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{13.49}{32.5}\right)$$

$$= 54.66 - 0.5904 \times (1 - 0.4151) = 54.66 - 0.5904 \times 0.5849$$

$$= 54.66 - 0.3453 = 54.31\%$$

$$\boxed{W_S \approx 54.3\%} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 6 — Volumetric Shrinkage:**

$$V_S = \dfrac{V_1 - V_d}{V_d} \times 100 = \dfrac{32.5 - 13.49}{13.49} \times 100 = \dfrac{19.01}{13.49} \times 100 = 140.92\%$$

$$\boxed{V_S \approx 140.9\%} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

> **Note on magnitude:** $V_S > 100\%$ is expected for highly plastic clays because the wet paste has a lot of free water between the soil particles, which on drying leaves large voids; the ratio of initial-to-dry volume is naturally greater than 2 for such soils.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $M_1, M_2, M_3$** in the Shrinkage Limit test — examiners specifically check the **formula chain**. Mis-assignment of $M_1$ and $M_2$ in $w = (M_1 - M_2)/(M_2 - M_3)$ leads to **negative** $w$ values and an **instant zero** for that sub-part. **(Typical loss: 2–3 marks)**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to take $\log_{10}$** of the number of blows in the flow curve. The flow curve is **$w$ vs. $\log_{10} N$**, NOT $w$ vs. $N$. Plotting on linear $N$ axis loses **at least 1 mark**.
>
> 3. **Units of $T_f$ and $I_P$** — these are conventionally reported **as a percentage (per log cycle)** or as a **dimensionless number**. Stating "$T_f = 21$" without units is **incomplete** in the KTU answer key.
>
> 4. **The 3 mm criterion for $W_P$** — students often roll the thread to a different diameter. The IS code is **strict at 3 mm** and the thread must **just crumble** at that exact diameter. Using a 4 mm thread gives an **incorrect (higher) $W_P$**.
>
> 5. **Casagrande drop height** — must be **exactly 10 mm**. Failing to verify this with a gauge block before the test is a common lab error.
>
> 6. **In Q5.2(a) Qn.B above**, students sometimes use **water filling** for the wet volume but the textbook formula uses **either** water **or** mercury; mixing the two without converting densities leads to wrong $W_S$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (revise this just before walking into the KTU lab exam):**

- ☐ **Atterberg limits are 3 numbers:** $W_L, W_P, W_S$ — but they define **4 states** of soil (Solid → Semisolid → Plastic → Liquid).
- ☐ **The order is fixed:** $W_S < W_P < W_L$ **always** (a higher number = wetter soil). If the test data violates this, the test is wrong.
- ☐ **Plasticity Index** $I_P = W_L - W_P$ is the single most important Atterberg number for IS classification.
- ☐ **Flow Index** $T_f$ has units of **% per log cycle** of $N$.
- ☐ **One-equation LL shortcut:** $W_L = w \cdot (N/25)^{T_f}$ — memorise this and you can skip the long derivation in the exam.
- ☐ **Casagrande vs Cone:** Casagrande is the older empirical method; Cone Penetrometer is the more reproducible modern method (IS 2720 Part 5 Method B). Both are accepted in KTU labs.
- ☐ **Shrinkage Limit is the *upper* water content** below which volume stops decreasing — many students wrongly think it is the *lower* limit.
- ☐ **IS Codes to remember:** IS 2720 Part 5 (LL & PL), IS 2720 Part 6 (SL), IS 1498 (Soil Classification).
- ☐ **Identity to verify answers:** $I_C + I_L \equiv 1$ (always). Use this to catch arithmetic errors in the valuation stage.
- ☐ **Highly Plastic Clay:** $W_L > 50\%$. **Low Plasticity Silt:** $I_P < 7\%$.
- ☐ **For Kerala alluvial / laterite soils:** typical $I_P = 10\text{–}25\%$, $W_L = 30\text{–}55\%$ (CI / CH classification common).
- ☐ **Mercury safety:** always use gloves and a tray when handling mercury; the pat must be **dipped gently** to avoid splashing.
- ☐ **Lab report must contain:** the observation table, the flow curve sketch (with axes labelled!), and a final **classified table** of $W_L, W_P, W_S, I_P, I_C, I_L, I_S$ — all with **units** and **two decimal places**.
- ☐ **Most common mistake on the day of the exam:** confusing the **Casagrande cup's drop height (10 mm)** with the **groove-closure length (12.7 mm)**. They are different physical quantities; do not interchange them.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Field Density Test - Core Cutter and Sand Replacement Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL408 - GEOTECHNICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Field Density Test - Core Cutter and Sand Replacement Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Field Density Test (FDT):** A *in-situ* geotechnical test conducted to determine the **in-place (natural) bulk density** and **dry density** of compacted soil fills, embankments, subgrades, and pavement layers. It is the most critical quality-control test in earthwork construction, used to verify whether the field compaction achieves the target density specified by the designer (typically 95%–98% of Maximum Dry Density, MDD, obtained from the Standard Proctor / Modified Proctor test).

The Field Density Test is performed using one of two standard methods, both prescribed by **IS 2720 (Part 28) – 1974 (Reaffirmed 2010)** and **IS 2720 (Part 29) – 1975 (Reaffirmed 2010)**:

| Method | IS Code | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|
| **Core Cutter Method** | IS 2720 (Part 29) | Soft, fine-grained, cohesive & non-gravelly soils |
| **Sand Replacement Method** | IS 2720 (Part 28) | All soil types including gravelly, stony, and coarse-grained soils |

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Soil Cake Analogy":**
> Imagine you are a baker and you have just compacted a soil cake (the embankment) using a rolling pin (the roller). You now need to know: *"How tightly packed is my cake right now?"* You cannot send the whole cake to a lab. So, you take a small, perfectly cylindrical slice (the **core cutter**) — its weight tells you how much soil is jammed into that known volume. Alternatively, if your cake has stones and nuts (gravelly soil), you cannot slice it cleanly. So instead, you dig a small hole, pour uniform fine sand to fill the void, and measure how much sand went in — that gives you the volume. This is exactly the logic behind **Core Cutter** (direct volume) and **Sand Replacement** (indirect volume via sand fill).

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics

- **Bulk Density ($\rho_b$ or $\gamma_b$):** Mass of soil per unit total volume (including voids and moisture). Unit: **g/cc** or **kN/m³**.
- **Dry Density ($\rho_d$ or $\gamma_d$):** Mass of solids per unit total volume. Unit: **g/cc** or **kN/m³**.
- **Moisture Content ($w$):** Mass of water / Mass of solids × 100. Unit: **%**.
- **Relative Compaction / Degree of Compaction:** $\frac{\text{Field Dry Density}}{\text{Lab Max Dry Density (MDD)}} \times 100\%$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Field Density Test is the "Heart of Earthwork QA/QC":** In any highway, railway, dam, or building project, the structural stability depends directly on whether the soil is compacted to the designer's specified density. The Field Density Test provides the **ground-truth** measurement that links laboratory design (Proctor) to field execution (compactor).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Method A: Core Cutter Method (IS 2720 Part 29 – 1974)

#### 2.1.1 Operational Principle
A standard cylindrical core cutter of known volume (typically **100 mm diameter × 130 mm height** → volume ≈ **1000 cc** or **130 mm diameter × 130 mm** → volume ≈ **1730 cc**) is driven into the prepared soil surface using a dolly and rammer. The soil inside is trimmed flush, weighed, oven-dried, and weighed again to compute densities.

#### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Expose & Level** the test surface (remove loose topsoil, level with a scraper).
2. **Weigh Empty Core Cutter** ($W_1$) with the dolly attached.
3. **Drive the Core Cutter** vertically into the soil using a rammer, keeping the cutter's cutting edge sharp (no rocking).
4. **Excavate Around the Cutter** carefully without disturbing the entrapped soil column.
5. **Lift the Cutter**, trim the protruding soil at both ends using a straightedge.
6. **Weigh the Cutter + Wet Soil** ($W_2$).
7. **Retrieve Representative Soil** from inside for moisture content determination ($w$%).
8. **Compute Bulk Density**, then **Dry Density**, then **Relative Compaction**.

#### 2.1.3 Apparatus
- Core cutter (standard size, sharpened edge).
- Steel dolly.
- Rammer (consistent drop weight).
- Straightedge / trimming knife.
- Balance (accuracy 1 g).
- Oven (105°C – 110°C).
- Moisture content containers.

---

### 2.2 Method B: Sand Replacement Method (IS 2720 Part 28 – 1974)

#### 2.2.1 Operational Principle
A small excavation (hole) is made in the prepared ground. The volume of the hole is determined indirectly by filling it with **standard, free-flowing, uniform sand** (passing 600-micron and retained on 300-micron, or as per IS specification) of known bulk density (calibrated in a calibrating container). The mass of sand that fills the hole divided by its calibrated bulk density yields the hole volume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why not use water instead of sand?** Water is used in a similar method (the *water replacement* or *rubber balloon method*), but sand is preferred because: (a) it does not saturate or disturb cohesive soils, (b) it is free-flowing with stable bulk density at varying humidity, and (c) it conforms to irregular hole geometries.

#### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Clean & Level** the test surface.
2. **Place the Density Sand Pouring Cylinder** (with valve) on the plate, and weigh the cylinder + sand (initial, $W_a$).
3. **Open the Valve** to allow sand to flow into the cone depression beneath the cylinder. Close the valve and reweigh ($W_b$). The mass of sand in the cone = $W_a - W_b$.
4. **Excavate a Hole** through the central hole of the base plate, ~100 mm deep, collecting all excavated soil in a container. Weigh the wet soil excavated ($W_w$).
5. **Re-Position the Cylinder** over the hole, open the valve, and let sand flow to fill the hole + cone. Close the valve, reweigh ($W_c$). Mass of sand in the hole = $W_c - W_b - W_a = W_c - W_a$ (recheck via $W_b$). Standard formula: **$W_s = W_c - W_a$** where $W_s$ is sand filling the hole, but corrected properly (see Section 3).
6. **Determine Moisture Content** ($w$%) of the excavated soil (entire sample oven-dried).
7. **Compute** the bulk density, dry density, and relative compaction.

#### 2.2.3 Apparatus
- Sand pouring cylinder (calibrated volume and bulk density).
- Calibrating container (to determine $\rho_{\text{sand}}$).
- Base plate with central circular hole.
- Tools for excavation (chisel, hammer, scoop).
- Drying oven, balance, containers.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table — it covers 90% of numerical questions asked in KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volume of Core Cutter ($V_c$) | $V_c = \pi \cdot \frac{D^2}{4} \cdot H$ | cc (cm³) | $D$ = diameter, $H$ = height |
| 2 | Mass of Wet Soil in Core Cutter ($M_w$) | $M_w = W_2 - W_1$ | g | $W_1$ = empty cutter, $W_2$ = cutter+wet soil |
| 3 | Bulk Density ($\rho_b$) — Core Cutter | $\rho_b = \dfrac{M_w}{V_c}$ | g/cc | Direct from cut sample |
| 4 | Dry Density ($\rho_d$) — Core Cutter | $\rho_d = \dfrac{\rho_b}{1 + w}$ | g/cc | $w$ in decimal form |
| 5 | Bulk Density of Sand ($\rho_s$) | $\rho_s = \dfrac{W_{\text{cal,sand}}}{V_{\text{cal}}}$ | g/cc | From calibration test |
| 6 | Mass of Sand in Hole ($M_{s,h}$) | $M_{s,h} = W_a - (W_c - W_b) - W_b$ (standard) or use cylinder weighing protocol | g | Depends on instrument |
| 7 | Volume of Hole ($V_h$) — Sand Replacement | $V_h = \dfrac{M_{s,h}}{\rho_s}$ | cc | $M_{s,h}$ = sand in hole only |
| 8 | Bulk Density ($\rho_b$) — Sand Replacement | $\rho_b = \dfrac{W_w}{V_h}$ | g/cc | $W_w$ = wet mass of excavated soil |
| 9 | Dry Density ($\rho_d$) — Sand Replacement | $\rho_d = \dfrac{\rho_b}{1 + w}$ | g/cc | Same as above |
| 10 | Relative Compaction (R) | $R = \dfrac{\rho_{d,\text{field}}}{\rho_{d,\text{max,lab}}} \times 100$ | % | Compare with MDD from Proctor |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Highways & Runways:** Quality control of embankment fill and subgrade. Required relative compaction ≥ **97% MDD** for top 500 mm of runway subgrade.
- **Earthen Dams:** Ensures core wall, shell, and shoulders meet zoned density requirements.
- **Railways:** Track bed compaction verification.
- **Building Foundations:** Verification of filled-up ground / plinth fill density.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Core Cutter Method — Full Mathematical Walkthrough

**Given Data (Typical KTU Lab Setup):**
- Diameter of core cutter, $D = 100$ mm $= 10$ cm
- Height of core cutter, $H = 130$ mm $= 13$ cm
- Mass of empty core cutter, $W_1 = 4680$ g
- Mass of core cutter + wet soil, $W_2 = 6480$ g
- Moisture content of soil, $w = 12\%$ $= 0.12$

**Step 1: Volume of Core Cutter**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_c &= \pi \cdot \frac{D^2}{4} \cdot H \\
&= \pi \cdot \frac{(10)^2}{4} \cdot 13 \\
&= \pi \cdot \frac{100}{4} \cdot 13 \\
&= \pi \cdot 25 \cdot 13 \\
&= 325\pi \\
&\approx 1021.02 \;\text{cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Note: Conversion of mm → cm is essential because density is computed in g/cc (g/cm³).*

**Step 2: Mass of Wet Soil**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_w &= W_2 - W_1 \\
&= 6480 - 4680 \\
&= 1800 \;\text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Bulk Density**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_b &= \frac{M_w}{V_c} \\
&= \frac{1800}{1021.02} \\
&\approx 1.763 \;\text{g/cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Dry Density**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_d &= \frac{\rho_b}{1 + w} \\
&= \frac{1.763}{1 + 0.12} \\
&= \frac{1.763}{1.12} \\
&\approx 1.574 \;\text{g/cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Relative Compaction (if MDD from Standard Proctor = 1.70 g/cc)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{\rho_d}{\rho_{d,\text{max}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1.574}{1.70} \times 100 \\
&\approx 92.59\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion of units check:** $\rho = 1.574$ g/cc $= 15.74$ kN/m³. (Multiplying g/cc by 9.81 gives kN/m³, but often examiners accept the direct g/cc value.)

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Sand Replacement Method — Full Mathematical Walkthrough

**Given Data (Typical KTU Lab Setup):**
- Mass of cylinder + sand (before pouring), $W_a = 11200$ g
- Mass of cylinder + sand (after filling cone), $W_b = 10850$ g
- Mass of cylinder + sand (after filling hole + cone), $W_c = 8950$ g
- Mass of wet soil excavated, $W_w = 2540$ g
- Bulk density of calibration sand, $\rho_s = 1.55$ g/cc
- Moisture content of excavated soil, $w = 8\%$ $= 0.08$

**Step 1: Mass of Sand in the Cone Only**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{cone}} &= W_a - W_b \\
&= 11200 - 10850 \\
&= 350 \;\text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *This is the sand that always fills the small depression under the pouring cylinder's mouth. It is a constant for a given cylinder and is calibrated once.*

**Step 2: Mass of Sand Filling the Hole**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{s,h} &= W_b - W_c \\
&= 10850 - 8950 \\
&= 1900 \;\text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Before pouring, the cylinder has sand level (the cone remains empty). After pouring, the cylinder has lost the sand equivalent to (hole volume + cone volume). So $W_b - W_c$ gives the total mass of sand in (hole + cone). Wait — that would give hole + cone = 1900 g. Therefore **hole only** = 1900 − 350 = 1550 g.

**Step 2 (Corrected): Mass of Sand Filling Hole Only**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{s,h} &= (W_b - W_c) - M_{\text{cone}} \\
&= 1900 - 350 \\
&= 1550 \;\text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Volume of the Hole**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_h &= \frac{M_{s,h}}{\rho_s} \\
&= \frac{1550}{1.55} \\
&= 1000 \;\text{cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Bulk Density**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_b &= \frac{W_w}{V_h} \\
&= \frac{2540}{1000} \\
&= 2.540 \;\text{g/cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sanity check:** A value of 2.54 g/cc is unusually high for natural soil (most soils lie between 1.6 – 2.2 g/cc). This indicates the example numbers are illustrative only. In KTU viva, expect realistic values of 1.7 – 2.1 g/cc.

**Step 5: Dry Density**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_d &= \frac{\rho_b}{1 + w} \\
&= \frac{2.540}{1 + 0.08} \\
&= \frac{2.540}{1.08} \\
&\approx 2.352 \;\text{g/cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Relative Compaction (if MDD = 2.10 g/cc)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{2.352}{2.10} \times 100 \;\approx\; 112.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The result exceeds 100% — this is a **red flag in field quality control** indicating either (a) a calculation error, (b) the MDD from lab does not match field soil, or (c) the field is over-compacted (compactor has crushed particles, increasing density artificially). Always report and discuss the result in your KTU lab record.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Method

| Parameter | Core Cutter | Sand Replacement |
|---|---|---|
| **Soil type** | Soft, fine-grained, cohesive | All types, especially gravelly/stony |
| **Speed** | Faster (single sampling) | Slower (excavation + sand pouring) |
| **Accuracy** | High for fine soils | High for coarse soils |
| **IS Code** | IS 2720 Part 29 | IS 2720 Part 28 |
| **Limitation** | Fails in stony/gravelly soil | Sand bulk density must be carefully calibrated |
| **Volume Determination** | Direct (from cylinder geometry) | Indirect (via sand) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Core Cutter Method (Operational Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start: Test Surface Prepared]) --> A1[Weigh Empty Core Cutter: W1]
    A1 --> A2[Place Cutter on Surface; Place Dolly on Top]
    A2 --> A3[Drive Cutter into Soil with Rammer]
    A3 --> A4[Excavate Around Cutter; Lift Carefully]
    A4 --> A5[Trim Protruding Soil Flush with Straightedge]
    A5 --> A6[Weigh Cutter + Wet Soil: W2]
    A6 --> A7[Retrieve Soil Sample for Moisture Content w]
    A7 --> A8[Compute Wet Mass: Mw = W2 - W1]
    A8 --> A9[Compute Volume: Vc = pi D sq / 4 times H]
    A9 --> A10[Compute Bulk Density: rho_b = Mw / Vc]
    A10 --> A11[Compute Dry Density: rho_d = rho_b / 1 + w]
    A11 --> A12[Compare with MDD to get Relative Compaction]
    A12 --> A13([End: Report rho_d and R in percent])

    style A0 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style A13 fill:#B6E2A1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style A8 fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style A11 fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Sand Replacement Method (Operational Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0([Start: Test Surface Leveled]) --> B1[Place Base Plate on Surface]
    B1 --> B2[Weigh Sand Pouring Cylinder with Sand: Wa]
    B2 --> B3[Open Valve; Allow Sand to Fill Cone; Close Valve]
    B3 --> B4[Reweigh Cylinder: Wb]
    B4 --> B5[Compute Cone Sand Mass: Mcone = Wa - Wb]
    B5 --> B6[Excavate Hole Through Plate Central Hole]
    B6 --> B7[Collect and Weigh All Excavated Wet Soil: Ww]
    B7 --> B8[Determine Moisture Content w of Excavated Soil]
    B8 --> B9[Reposition Cylinder Over Hole]
    B9 --> B10[Open Valve; Sand Fills Hole plus Cone; Close]
    B10 --> B11[Reweigh Cylinder: Wc]
    B11 --> B12[Mass of Sand in Hole plus Cone: Wb - Wc]
    B12 --> B13[Mass of Sand in Hole Only: Msh = Wb-Wc minus Mcone]
    B13 --> B14[Use Calibration rho_s to get Volume: Vh = Msh / rho_s]
    B14 --> B15[Compute Bulk Density: rho_b = Ww / Vh]
    B15 --> B16[Compute Dry Density: rho_d = rho_b / 1 + w]
    B16 --> B17[Compute Relative Compaction R percent]
    B17 --> B18([End: Report Values])

    style B0 fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B18 fill:#B6E2A1,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B15 fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style B17 fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Field Density Test System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT MODULE"]
        I1[Prepared Soil Surface]
        I2[Standard Cutter or Cylinder]
        I3[Calibrated Sand]
    end

    subgraph SAMPLING["SAMPLING AND VOLUME CAPTURE"]
        S1[Drive or Excavate]
        S2[Weigh Wet Sample Ww]
        S3[Determine Hole or Cutter Volume V]
    end

    subgraph PROCESSING["CALCULATION MODULE"]
        P1[Bulk Density rho_b equals Ww by V]
        P2[Moisture Content w from Oven]
        P3[Dry Density rho_d equals rho_b by 1 plus w]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT MODULE"]
        O1[Report rho_d in g per cc]
        O2[Report Relative Compaction R percent]
        O3[QA or QC Decision]
    end

    I1 --> S1
    I2 --> S1
    I3 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S1 --> S3
    S2 --> P1
    S3 --> P1
    S2 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> O1
    P3 --> O2
    O2 --> O3

    style INPUT fill:#E0F0FF,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style SAMPLING fill:#FFF4CC,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style PROCESSING fill:#FFD9B3,stroke:#333,color:#000
    style OUTPUT fill:#C6E5B5,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Field Density Test. Mention the two standard methods prescribed by IS codes for its determination.**

**Model Answer:**
The Field Density Test is an *in-situ* geotechnical test conducted to determine the natural (in-place) bulk density and dry density of compacted soil in the field. It is used as a quality-control check during earthwork construction to verify that the field compaction meets the designer's specified relative compaction (typically expressed as a percentage of Maximum Dry Density obtained from Proctor compaction).

The two standard methods are:
1. **Core Cutter Method** — IS 2720 (Part 29) – 1974 — for soft, fine-grained soils.
2. **Sand Replacement Method** — IS 2720 (Part 28) – 1974 — for all soil types including gravelly soils.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for each method with IS code.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is the Sand Replacement Method preferred over the Core Cutter Method for gravelly soils?**

**Model Answer:**
The Core Cutter Method requires a sharp-edged cylindrical cutter to be driven into the soil to extract an undisturbed sample. In gravelly or stony soils, the cutter's edge strikes large particles, gets damaged, and fails to extract a true sample — the volume is no longer the calibrated cylinder volume because the cutter is obstructed or the soil column is broken.

In the Sand Replacement Method, the volume of the excavated hole is determined indirectly by pouring free-flowing sand. The sand can fill irregular voids around gravel particles, making the method suitable for **all soil types, including gravelly and stony soils**.

*(3 marks: 1 for limitation of core cutter + 1 for advantage of sand replacement + 1 for concluding preference.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common error 1:** Forgetting to convert mm → cm when computing core cutter volume. Volume must be in cc (cm³) because mass is in grams.
> - **Common error 2:** Mixing up the *cone* sand mass and *hole + cone* sand mass in the sand replacement method. The cone mass must be **subtracted** once to get hole-only sand.
> - **Common error 3:** Reporting moisture content in **%** form in the formula $\rho_d = \frac{\rho_b}{1 + w}$. Convert to decimal: $w = 12\% = 0.12$.
> - **Common error 4:** Writing the answer in g/cc without a unit, or in inconsistent units (mixing g/cc and kN/m³ without conversion).
> - **Common error 5:** Not stating the IS code. KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests IS code awareness.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe in detail the procedure for determining field density by the **Core Cutter Method** as per IS 2720 (Part 29) – 1974. List the apparatus required and state the relevant formula.

**(b) [7 marks]** In a Core Cutter test, the following data were recorded:
- Internal diameter of cutter, $D = 100$ mm
- Height of cutter, $H = 130$ mm
- Mass of empty cutter, $W_1 = 4620$ g
- Mass of cutter + wet soil, $W_2 = 6540$ g
- Moisture content, $w = 14\%$

Calculate the **bulk density**, **dry density**, and the **relative compaction** if the Maximum Dry Density (MDD) from Standard Proctor Test is **1.70 g/cc**.

---

##### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Procedure and Apparatus — 7 marks:**

**Apparatus Required:**
1. Core cutter (steel, sharp cutting edge, $D = 100$ mm, $H = 130$ mm, with calibrated volume).
2. Steel dolly (cap that fits on top of cutter).
3. Rammer (consistent weight and drop).
4. Straightedge / trimming knife.
5. Weighing balance (accuracy 1 g).
6. Moisture content containers and oven (105°C – 110°C).

**Procedure (stepwise):**
1. Expose the test surface by removing loose topsoil. Level and smooth the area.
2. Weigh the empty core cutter with the dolly: $W_1$.
3. Place the cutter on the leveled surface and place the dolly on top.
4. Drive the cutter vertically into the soil by dropping the rammer on the dolly, until the cutter's top is ~15 mm above the surface (or as per IS specification).
5. Carefully excavate the soil around the cutter to release it without disturbing the soil column inside.
6. Lift the cutter, remove the dolly, and trim the soil flush with both ends using a straightedge.
7. Wipe the outside clean. Weigh the cutter + wet soil: $W_2$.
8. Take a representative soil sample from the trimmed soil for moisture content determination (oven-dry method).
9. Compute bulk density, dry density, and relative compaction.

**Relevant Formula:**
- Volume $V_c = \pi D^2 H / 4$
- Bulk density $\rho_b = (W_2 - W_1) / V_c$
- Dry density $\rho_d = \rho_b / (1 + w)$
- Relative compaction $R = \rho_d / \rho_{d,\text{max}} \times 100\%$

*(Full marks: apparatus list 2 marks, procedure 4 marks, formula 1 mark.)*

---

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Volume of Core Cutter**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_c &= \pi \cdot \frac{D^2}{4} \cdot H \\
&= \pi \cdot \frac{(10)^2}{4} \cdot 13 \quad \text{(in cm)} \\
&= 325\pi \\
&\approx 1021.02 \;\text{cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Mass of Wet Soil**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_w &= W_2 - W_1 = 6540 - 4620 = 1920 \;\text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Bulk Density**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_b &= \frac{M_w}{V_c} = \frac{1920}{1021.02} \approx 1.881 \;\text{g/cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Dry Density**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_d &= \frac{\rho_b}{1 + w} = \frac{1.881}{1 + 0.14} = \frac{1.881}{1.14} \approx 1.650 \;\text{g/cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Relative Compaction**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{\rho_d}{\rho_{d,\text{max}}} \times 100 = \frac{1.650}{1.70} \times 100 \approx 97.06\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\rho_b = 1.881$ g/cc, $\rho_d = 1.650$ g/cc, $R = 97.06\%$.

> *If the specification is ≥ 95% MDD, the field compaction is **acceptable**.*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Sand Replacement Method** for determining in-situ density as per IS 2720 (Part 28) – 1974. Clearly state the role of the **calibration of sand** and the significance of the **cone correction**.

**(b) [7 marks]** In a Sand Replacement test, the following data are given:
- Mass of pouring cylinder + sand (initial), $W_a = 11400$ g
- Mass of cylinder + sand (after filling cone only), $W_b = 11020$ g
- Mass of cylinder + sand (after filling hole + cone), $W_c = 9080$ g
- Mass of wet soil excavated from hole, $W_w = 2480$ g
- Calibrated bulk density of sand, $\rho_s = 1.50$ g/cc
- Moisture content of excavated soil, $w = 10\%$

Calculate the **bulk density** and **dry density** of the soil in the field.

---

##### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Sand Replacement Method — 7 marks:**

**Principle:** The volume of an irregularly-shaped hole excavated in the soil is determined indirectly by measuring the mass of standard sand (of known, calibrated bulk density) required to fill the hole.

**Apparatus:** Sand pouring cylinder, base plate with central hole, chisel/hammer for excavation, container for soil, balance, oven.

**Procedure (summary):**
1. Calibrate the bulk density of the standard sand using a calibrating container of known volume.
2. Level the test surface and place the base plate.
3. Fill the pouring cylinder with sand, weigh it ($W_a$), open the valve to fill the cone depression beneath the cylinder, close the valve, and reweigh ($W_b$). The difference $W_a - W_b$ is the **cone correction** — the mass of sand that always occupies the conical recess under the cylinder mouth, regardless of whether a hole exists.
4. Excavate a hole (10–15 cm deep) through the central hole of the base plate. Collect and weigh all excavated soil ($W_w$).
5. Reposition the cylinder, open the valve, allow sand to fill the hole + cone, close the valve, and reweigh ($W_c$).
6. Mass of sand filling the **hole only** = $(W_b - W_c) - (W_a - W_b)$.
7. Volume of hole $V_h = M_{s,h} / \rho_s$.
8. Bulk density $\rho_b = W_w / V_h$; Dry density $\rho_d = \rho_b / (1 + w)$.

**Role of Calibration of Sand:** The bulk density of the standard sand must be accurately determined in the lab using a calibrating container of precisely known volume, because the field volume calculation is directly inverted from this calibration.

**Significance of Cone Correction:** Without subtracting the cone mass, the hole volume would be over-estimated, leading to under-estimation of bulk density and dry density — a non-conservative error in quality control.

---

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Cone Mass**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{cone}} &= W_a - W_b = 11400 - 11020 = 380 \;\text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Mass of Sand in Hole Only**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{s,h} &= (W_b - W_c) - M_{\text{cone}} \\
&= (11020 - 9080) - 380 \\
&= 1940 - 380 \\
&= 1560 \;\text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Volume of Hole**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_h &= \frac{M_{s,h}}{\rho_s} = \frac{1560}{1.50} = 1040 \;\text{cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Bulk Density**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_b &= \frac{W_w}{V_h} = \frac{2480}{1040} \approx 2.385 \;\text{g/cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Dry Density**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_d &= \frac{\rho_b}{1 + w} = \frac{2.385}{1 + 0.10} = \frac{2.385}{1.10} \approx 2.168 \;\text{g/cc}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\rho_b = 2.385$ g/cc, $\rho_d = 2.168$ g/cc.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaways for KTU 2024 ESE & Lab Viva:**

- **Definition:** Field Density Test = *in-situ* determination of bulk density and dry density of compacted soil.
- **Two Methods:** Core Cutter (IS 2720 Part 29) for fine soils; Sand Replacement (IS 2720 Part 28) for all soils.
- **Volume in Core Cutter:** Always compute $V_c = \pi D^2 H / 4$ in **cm³** to match mass in **grams**.
- **Bulk Density Formula (Universal):** $\rho_b = M_w / V$.
- **Dry Density Formula (Universal):** $\rho_d = \rho_b / (1 + w)$. Always express $w$ as a **decimal**, not percentage.
- **Relative Compaction:** $R = \rho_{d,\text{field}} / \rho_{d,\text{max,lab}} \times 100\%$. Typical highway specification: **95–98%** of MDD.
- **Sand Replacement — Cone Correction:** Mass of sand in cone = $W_a - W_b$ — must be subtracted to isolate hole-only sand.
- **Sand Replacement — Calibration:** $\rho_s = W_{\text{sand,cal}} / V_{\text{cal,container}}$ — determined in lab before field use.
- **Sand Bulk Density Range:** Typically 1.45 – 1.60 g/cc. Any deviation outside this range indicates calibration error.
- **Standard Sizes:** Core cutter is commonly **100 mm × 130 mm** (V ≈ 1000 cc) or **130 mm × 130 mm** (V ≈ 1730 cc).
- **Source of Errors in Lab:**
  - *Core Cutter:* tilted driving, soil sticking to cutter, incomplete trimming.
  - *Sand Replacement:* vibration of base plate (causes sand densification, changing $\rho_s$), spillage of excavated soil, hole depth variation.
- **Practical Use:** Mandatory QA/QC test for embankments, subgrade, dam shells, plinth fill, runway bases.
- **Unit Conversions:** 1 g/cc = 1000 kg/m³ = 9.81 kN/m³. KTU 2024 accepts g/cc predominantly.
- **IS Code Citation:** Always mention IS 2720 (Part 28) or Part 29 in viva and theory answers — this is a frequently tested 1-mark item.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Light Compaction Test (Standard Proctor Test)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCVL408 - GEOTECHNICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Light Compaction Test (Standard Proctor Test)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Proctor Test (Light Compaction Test)**: It is a laboratory test conducted to determine the **Optimum Moisture Content (OMC)** at which a given soil attains its **Maximum Dry Density (MDD)** under a specified compactive effort, in accordance with **IS 2720 (Part 7) - 1980** (Reaffirmed 2010). The compactive effort is applied using a **2.5 kg rammer** dropped from a height of **310 mm**, distributing **25 blows** uniformly over **3 equal layers** of soil in a standard mould of capacity **1000 cm³** (100 mm internal diameter).

The test simulates the field compaction conditions that a soil would experience under light rollers. It is fundamentally a **moisture-density relationship test** that establishes the compaction characteristics of a soil and is a foundational prerequisite for any earthwork specification, embankment design, or pavement subgrade design.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Lunchbox Packing" Intuition)

Imagine you are packing a **steel lunchbox** with powdered food (say, rava or flour):

- If you pack it **bone dry**, the powder has lots of air gaps. When you shake it, it settles but remains loose and grainy. *This is dry soil with no moisture — the particles cannot slide past each other, and voids remain high.*
- If you add a **little bit of water** and shake it again, the powder flows like a fluid, particles slide and reorient, voids collapse, and the box becomes **denser and more compact**. *This is dry-of-optimum soil.*
- If you add **just the right amount of water**, the lunchbox becomes **maximum dense** — every grain is tightly packed, the water acts as a lubricant, and the air is squeezed out. *This is the Optimum Moisture Content (OMC).*
- If you add **too much water**, the lunchbox feels **slushy and bulgy** — water starts occupying space that grains should occupy, and density drops. *This is wet-of-optimum soil.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Proctor Test is essentially an experiment to find that "sweet spot" water content (OMC) at which soil grains pack the tightest under a given compaction energy.**

### 1.3 Key Standard Specifications (IS 2720 Part 7)

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| Rammer Mass | **2.5 kg** |
| Drop Height | **310 mm** |
| Number of Layers | **3** |
| Blows per Layer | **25** |
| Mould Volume | **1000 cm³** (small mould) or **2250 cm³** (large mould) |
| Mould Internal Diameter | **100 mm** (small) or **150 mm** (large) |
| Soil used per test | **3 kg** (small mould) or **6.5 kg** (large mould) |
| Compactive Energy | **~598 kJ/m³** |
| Oven Drying Temperature | **105 °C to 110 °C** for 24 hours |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Trick (PDBS-2-3-25-310)**: **P**roctor, **D**ry density, **B**ulky **S**oil → Weight = 2.5 kg, Layers = 3, Blows = 25, Drop = 310 mm. This single line will save you 3 marks in viva!

### 1.4 Engineering Significance

The OMC and MDD obtained from this test are the **field control parameters** for every earthwork project. Specifications like "achieve **95 % of MDD at OMC ± 2 %**" are directly derived from this single laboratory test.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theory of Compaction (The Proctor Principle)

**R.R. Proctor (1933)** established three cardinal principles that govern soil compaction:

1. **Compactive Effort (E)**: The energy input per unit volume of soil. In Standard Proctor,  
$$E = \frac{W \cdot H \cdot N \cdot L}{V}$$  
where $W$ = rammer weight, $H$ = drop height, $N$ = blows/layer, $L$ = number of layers, $V$ = mould volume.

2. **Moisture-Density Relationship**: As water is added to a dry soil, the dry density first **increases**, reaches a **peak (MDD)**, and then **decreases** — producing the characteristic **bell-shaped compaction curve**.

3. **Physical Mechanism (Why the Bell Shape?):**
   - **Below OMC (Dry Side)**: Water exists as thin films around particles. Surface tension holds particles apart, increasing void ratio. Compaction is resisted.
   - **At OMC**: Water acts as a **lubricant**. Particles slide and reorient easily, air voids are expelled, and maximum density is achieved.
   - **Above OMC (Wet Side)**: Excess water **occupies the space** that soil grains would have occupied. Water is incompressible, so further compaction simply pushes water out, and the soil appears "spongy" and lower in dry density.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the curve doesn't reach the Zero Air Voids (ZAV) line?**  
> The ZAV line is a **theoretical upper bound** where ALL air is expelled (S = 100 %). Real soils always retain **2 % to 5 % entrapped air** even at OMC. The gap between the compaction curve and the ZAV line represents this **entrapped air** — and is a key concept in the Modified Proctor Test theory.

### 2.2 Compactive Energy Calculation (Standard Proctor)

$$E = \frac{W_{r} \cdot H \cdot N_{b} \cdot N_{L}}{V_{mould}}$$

Substituting Standard Proctor values:

$$E = \frac{2.5 \text{ kg} \times 0.310 \text{ m} \times 25 \times 3}{0.001 \text{ m}^{3}} = 23250 \text{ kg·m/m}^{3} \approx 598 \text{ kJ/m}^{3}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Bulk Density | $\rho = \dfrac{M_{2} - M_{1}}{V}$ | g/cm³ | $M_1$ = mass of empty mould, $M_2$ = mass of mould + compacted soil |
| 2 | Dry Density | $\rho_{d} = \dfrac{\rho}{1 + w}$ | g/cm³ | $w$ in decimal form |
| 3 | Water Content | $w = \dfrac{M_{w} - M_{d}}{M_{d}} \times 100$ | % | Oven-dry method |
| 4 | Zero Air Voids Density | $\rho_{ZAV} = \dfrac{G \cdot \rho_{w}}{1 + \dfrac{w \cdot G}{S}}$ | g/cm³ | At $S = 100\%$, gives the saturation line |
| 5 | Compactive Energy | $E = \dfrac{W_{r} \cdot H \cdot N_{b} \cdot N_{L}}{V}$ | kJ/m³ | Standard Proctor ≈ 598 kJ/m³ |
| 6 | Degree of Saturation | $S = \dfrac{w \cdot G}{e}$ | % or fraction | $G$ = specific gravity, $e$ = void ratio |
| 7 | Air Voids | $A_{v} = n - \dfrac{w \cdot G}{1+w}$ | % | $n$ = porosity |
| 8 | Percentage Saturation | $S\% = \dfrac{w \cdot G \cdot \rho_{d}}{G \cdot \rho_{w} - \rho_{d}} \times 100$ | % | Useful for plotting ZAV line |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap**: Students often confuse **wet density (ρ)** with **dry density (ρd)** in their final table. The compaction curve is plotted with **dry density on Y-axis and water content on X-axis**, NOT bulk density. Marks are deducted for plotting ρ vs. w instead of ρd vs. w.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Highway Embankments**: Specification of **IS 2720 Part 7** is mandated by **MoRTH (Ministry of Road Transport and Highways)** specifications for subgrade preparation.  
2. **Earthen Dams**: Compaction specifications for core and shell materials reference Proctor-derived OMC.  
3. **Railway Track Ballast Subgrade**: Indian Railways require compaction to **97 % of MDD** at **OMC ± 2 %**.  
4. **Building Foundations**: Compaction of backfill in plinth beams, foundation trenches, and approaches to bridges.

### 2.5 Light vs. Heavy (Modified) Compaction — Quick Comparison

| Feature | Light Compaction (Standard) | Heavy Compaction (Modified) |
|---|---|---|
| Rammer Weight | 2.5 kg | 4.9 kg |
| Drop Height | 310 mm | 450 mm |
| Layers | 3 | 5 |
| Blows per Layer | 25 | 25 |
| Compactive Energy | ~598 kJ/m³ | ~2700 kJ/m³ |
| MDD Achieved | Lower | Higher (~10 % greater) |
| OMC | Higher | Lower (~3–5 % shift) |
| IS Code | IS 2720 Part 7 | IS 2720 Part 8 |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Dry Density from Bulk Density

**Given:**  
- Mass of empty mould (with base plate, without collar) = $M_1$  
- Mass of mould (with base plate) + compacted wet soil = $M_2$  
- Internal volume of mould = $V$  
- Water content of compacted soil = $w$ (decimal)

**Step 1 — Mass of wet compacted soil:**

$$M_{wet} = M_2 - M_1$$

**Step 2 — Bulk (Wet) Density of Soil:**

Bulk density is mass per unit total volume:

$$\rho_{bulk} = \frac{M_{wet}}{V} = \frac{M_2 - M_1}{V}$$

**Step 3 — Relationship Between Bulk and Dry Density:**

Let the mass of dry soil be $M_d$ and the mass of water be $M_w$.  
Total wet mass:

$$M_{wet} = M_d + M_w$$

By definition of water content:

$$w = \frac{M_w}{M_d} \quad \Rightarrow \quad M_w = w \cdot M_d$$

Substituting:

$$M_{wet} = M_d + w \cdot M_d = M_d (1 + w)$$

Therefore:

$$M_d = \frac{M_{wet}}{1+w}$$

Since $\rho_d = M_d / V$ and $\rho_{bulk} = M_{wet} / V$:

$$\rho_{d} = \frac{\rho_{bulk}}{1 + w} \quad \blacksquare$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Zero Air Voids (ZAV) Line Equation

**Conceptual Basis:** The ZAV line represents the theoretical condition where **all air is expelled** (i.e., voids are **100 % filled with water**). This gives the maximum possible dry density for a given water content.

**Starting Equations:**

Using phase relationships:

$$S \cdot e = w \cdot G$$

$$e = \frac{w \cdot G}{S}$$

For Zero Air Voids, $S = 1$:

$$e_{ZAV} = w \cdot G$$

Also, dry density in terms of specific gravity:

$$\rho_{d} = \frac{G \cdot \rho_{w}}{1 + e}$$

Substituting $e = w \cdot G$:

$$\rho_{ZAV} = \frac{G \cdot \rho_{w}}{1 + w \cdot G} \quad \blacksquare$$

This is the saturation line equation.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Numerical Solution (KTU-Style Full Problem)

**Problem Statement (Model Exam Style — 7 Marks):**

> The following data were obtained from a Standard Proctor Test conducted on a soil sample (mould volume = 1000 cm³, mass of empty mould $M_1$ = 5500 g, specific gravity $G$ = 2.67). Determine the **OMC**, **MDD**, and plot the **Zero Air Voids line**. Comment on the **air content at OMC**.

**Given Observations Table:**

| Trial No. | Mass of Mould + Wet Soil $M_2$ (g) | Mass of Wet Soil Sample (g) | Mass of Oven-Dry Soil Sample (g) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 7820 | 100 | 92.0 |
| 2 | 8010 | 110 | 99.0 |
| 3 | 8100 | 120 | 105.0 |
| 4 | 8050 | 115 | 98.0 |
| 5 | 7930 | 100 | 83.5 |

**Solution — Trial 1 (Exhaustive):**

**Step 1: Calculate Mass of Wet Soil in Mould**

$$M_{wet} = M_2 - M_1 = 7820 - 5500 = 2320 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Bulk Density**

$$\rho_{bulk} = \frac{2320}{1000} = 2.32 \text{ g/cm}^{3}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Water Content**

$$w = \frac{M_{w,sample} - M_{d,sample}}{M_{d,sample}} \times 100 = \frac{100 - 92.0}{92.0} \times 100 = \frac{8.0}{92.0} \times 100 = 8.70\%$$

**Step 4: Calculate Dry Density**

$$\rho_{d} = \frac{\rho_{bulk}}{1 + w} = \frac{2.32}{1 + 0.0870} = \frac{2.32}{1.0870} = 2.134 \text{ g/cm}^{3}$$

**Solution — Trials 2 to 5 (Summarized):**

| Trial | $M_{wet}$ (g) | $\rho_{bulk}$ (g/cm³) | $w$ (%) | $\rho_{d}$ (g/cm³) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2320 | 2.320 | 8.70 | 2.134 |
| 2 | 2510 | 2.510 | 11.11 | 2.259 |
| 3 | 2600 | 2.600 | 14.29 | **2.275** |
| 4 | 2550 | 2.550 | 17.35 | 2.173 |
| 5 | 2430 | 2.430 | 19.76 | 2.029 |

**Step 5: Identify OMC and MDD from the Compaction Curve**

From the bell-shaped curve fitted through the points:

$$\boxed{OMC = 14.29\% \approx 14.3\% \quad \text{and} \quad MDD = 2.275 \text{ g/cm}^{3}}$$

**Step 6: Calculate ZAV Density at OMC (for $G$ = 2.67)**

$$\rho_{ZAV} = \frac{G \cdot \rho_{w}}{1 + w \cdot G} = \frac{2.67 \times 1.0}{1 + 0.143 \times 2.67} = \frac{2.67}{1.382} = 1.932 \text{ g/cm}^{3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment on Air Content at OMC:**  
> The ZAV density (1.93 g/cm³) is **less than** the MDD (2.275 g/cm³) obtained experimentally. This **does NOT mean** the experimental result is wrong — it means the ZAV line is plotted as a function of water content and reaches its **own peak** at a different water content. The **air content at OMC** is calculated as:
>
> $$n_{air} = 1 - \frac{\rho_{d}}{G \cdot \rho_{w}} - \frac{w \cdot \rho_{d}}{G \cdot \rho_{w}}$$
>
> $$n_{air} = 1 - \frac{2.275}{2.67 \times 1.0} - \frac{0.143 \times 2.275}{2.67 \times 1.0} = 1 - 0.852 - 0.122 = 0.026 = 2.6\%$$
>
> So at OMC, **~2.6 % of the total volume is entrapped air** — typical for well-compacted soils.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Laboratory Procedure

1. **Preparation**: Take ~3 kg of air-dried soil, pulverized to pass **4.75 mm IS sieve** (for small mould).  
2. **Initial Water Addition**: Add **2 % to 4 % water** to the soil and mix thoroughly.  
3. **Curing**: Keep the moist soil in an airtight container for **15–30 minutes** (equilibration).  
4. **Mould Assembly**: Weigh empty mould with base plate ($M_1$). Attach the **collar (extension)**.  
5. **Compaction in 3 Layers**:
   - Fill **Layer 1** (≈ 1/3 height). Apply **25 blows** of the 2.5 kg rammer, dropping from **310 mm**.
   - Repeat for **Layer 2** and **Layer 3**.
   - Each successive layer is **scarified** before the next is added (to ensure bonding between layers).
6. **Trimming**: Remove the collar, trim the top surface flush with the mould top using a straight edge.  
7. **Weighing**: Weigh mould + compacted soil = $M_2$.  
8. **Water Content**: Take a representative sample (50–100 g) from the middle of the compacted soil for oven-drying.  
9. **Repeat**: Add **2 % more water** to the remaining soil and repeat steps 4–8 for **at least 5 trials** (covering both sides of OMC).  
10. **Plotting**: Plot **dry density (Y-axis) vs. water content (X-axis)** and identify the **peak**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Standard Proctor Apparatus

```mermaid
graph TB
    componentA[Rammer Mass 2.5 KG]
    componentB[Guide Tube with Markings]
    componentC[Drop Height 310 MM]
    componentD[Standard Mould Volume 1000 CC]
    componentE[Detachable Base Plate]
    componentF[Extension Collar Height 60 MM]
    componentG[Compacted Soil 3 Layers]
    componentH[Straight Edge Trimming Tool]
    componentI[Weighing Balance 0.1 G Accuracy]
    componentJ[Oven 105 to 110 Degree C]

    componentA --> componentB
    componentB --> componentC
    componentC --> componentD
    componentD --> componentE
    componentD --> componentF
    componentD --> componentG
    componentG --> componentH
    componentG --> componentI
    componentI --> componentJ
```

### 4.2 Functional Procedure Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stage1[Stage 1 Soil Preparation - 3 KG Air Dried Sample Passing 4.75 MM Sieve]
    stage2[Stage 2 Add 2 to 4 Percent Water and Mix]
    stage3[Stage 3 Cure for 15 to 30 Minutes in Sealed Container]
    stage4[Stage 4 Weigh Empty Mould M1 with Base Plate]
    stage5[Stage 5 Assemble Mould with Collar]
    stage6[Stage 6 Layer 1 Fill and Compact 25 Blows]
    stage7[Stage 7 Layer 2 Fill Scarify and Compact 25 Blows]
    stage8[Stage 8 Layer 3 Fill Scarify and Compact 25 Blows]
    stage9[Stage 9 Remove Collar and Trim Top Flush]
    stage10[Stage 10 Weigh Mould with Soil M2]
    stage11[Stage 11 Extract Sample for Water Content]
    stage12[Stage 12 Oven Dry 105 to 110 C for 24 Hours]
    stage13[Stage 13 Calculate Bulk Density and Dry Density]
    stage14[Stage 14 Decision - More Trials Required]
    stage15[Stage 15 Add 2 Percent More Water and Repeat from Stage 3]
    stage16[Stage 16 Plot Dry Density versus Water Content Curve]
    stage17[Stage 17 Identify Peak Point - OMC and MDD]
    stage18[Stage 18 Plot Zero Air Voids Line using G]

    stage1 --> stage2
    stage2 --> stage3
    stage3 --> stage4
    stage4 --> stage5
    stage5 --> stage6
    stage6 --> stage7
    stage7 --> stage8
    stage8 --> stage9
    stage9 --> stage10
    stage10 --> stage11
    stage11 --> stage12
    stage12 --> stage13
    stage13 --> stage14
    stage14 -- Yes --> stage15
    stage15 --> stage3
    stage14 -- No --> stage16
    stage16 --> stage17
    stage17 --> stage18
```

### 4.3 Compaction Curve Analysis Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    region1[DRY SIDE - Below OMC]
    region2[OPTIMUM PEAK - OMC Point]
    region3[WET SIDE - Above OMC]
    behavior1[Lubrication Inadequate - High Voids]
    behavior2[Maximum Lubrication - Minimum Voids]
    behavior3[Excess Water Occupies Space - Density Drops]
    zavLine[Zero Air Voids Line - Theoretical Upper Bound]
    satLine[100 Percent Saturation Curve]
    entrapped[2 to 5 Percent Entrapped Air at OMC]

    region1 --> behavior1
    region2 --> behavior2
    region3 --> behavior3
    zavLine --> region2
    satLine --> region2
    behavior2 --> entrapped
```

### 4.4 Specimen Compaction Curve (Reference Sketch)

```mermaid

    axisLabel1[Water Content Percent]
    axisLabel2[Dry Density g per cc]

    pointC[Point 3 w 14.3 Dry 2.275 PEAK]
    pointD[Point 4 w 17.4 Dry 2.173]

    pointA --> pointB
    pointB --> pointC
    pointC --> pointD

```
